input.title: Fluent work(ctx) helper: shorter check-the-work rules, cached context reads
input.head: claude/check-work-test-functions-2fctkv
input.body:
Closes #392.

## What

Adds `engine/checks/helpers/work.mjs` — a fluent, null-safe check-the-work surface over `ctx` — and rewrites the check-the-work rule modules against it, trimming their preamble comments to the load-bearing whys.

The API (mechanism only; a rule still owns its patterns, file filters, and failure text):

- `work(ctx).conversation().ownerTurns().last()` — transcript chain that never needs a guard ladder: no transcript ⇒ empty turns; `.last()` on empty ⇒ a null-object turn whose predicates (`classified()`, `classes()`, `reply()`, `time()`, `excerpt(n)`) all answer emptily. `Turns` is an Array subclass, so `.filter(...)` stays chainable and keeps `.last()`.
- `jsonPair(file)` — `{ head, base }` parsed JSON at head and the scoping base; absent/unparsable side ⇒ `null`. Replaces per-rule double try/catch.
- `deadLinks()` / `danglingReferences(tolerated)` — the two reference-integrity walks (relative-link resolution; deleted-path mentions with the widen-to-path-token innocence test).
- `addedLines()`, `branchCommits()` (with parsed `.time`), `introducedMerges()`, `onDefaultBranch()`, `filesContaining(needle)`.

Rules rewritten: `comment-classification`, `squash-merge-history`, `reference-integrity`, `task-lifecycle`, `feature-requirements-first`, `node/earn-each-dependency`, `cer/permission-added-store-issue`, `google-client-id-single-origin`. Net: −195/+232 lines including the new helper; the rule bodies themselves shrink substantially (e.g. `comment-classification`'s `run` is now 2 lines of logic; `reference-integrity` drops its ~50 lines of file-local helpers).

## Performance

`ctx.read` and `ctx.readBase` are now cached for the sweep — checks are read-only over an immutable snapshot, many rules re-read the same files, and each `readBase` call was a `git show` subprocess.

## Verification

- Full CI suite (489 tests) green before and after the rewrite — the rules' fixture tests pin behavior, so the refactor is proven behavior-preserving.
- See-it-fail on the new helper via mutation: weakening `Turns.last` (5 failures), `danglingReferences` (2 failures), and `jsonPair`'s base side (6 failures) each made the existing fixtures bite; restored byte-identical afterwards.

Also removed three broken `.claude/skills/` symlinks left dangling by the pre-#385 top-level `skills/` layout (untracked, targets gone).

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)

https://claude.ai/code/session_015EGXi3y8hZNfS7A9csREv1

content.text: {"id":"4107354760","url":"https://github.com/missingbulb/Claudinite/pull/393"}

Comment class: feature.

Done — [PR #393](https://github.com/missingbulb/Claudinite/pull/393), tracking [#392](https://github.com/missingbulb/Claudinite/issues/392), all 489 tests green.

**The `work` object** (`engine/checks/helpers/work.mjs`): a fluent, null-safe surface over `ctx`, exactly in the direction of your example — every accessor absorbs its own guard, so no rule ever writes `if (!entries) return []` again. `comment-classification`'s entire logic is now:

```js
const last = work(ctx).conversation().ownerTurns().last();
if (!last.exists || last.classified()) return [];
```

`.last()` on an empty chain returns a null-object turn whose predicates all answer emptily, and `Turns` is an Array subclass so `.filter(...)` stays chainable — `feature-requirements-first` finds its trigger with `w.conversation().ownerTurns().filter(t => t.classes().has('feature')).last()`.

**The sweep over the other check-the-work rules**, all rewritten with comments trimmed to load-bearing whys:

- `reference-integrity` — its two loops collapsed to `[...w.deadLinks().map(finding), ...w.danglingReferences(migrationGoverns).map(finding)]`; the ~50 lines of file-local widen-token/innocence helpers moved into the helper as mechanism, while the migration-tolerance predicate (policy) stayed in the rule as 5 lines.
- `earn-each-dependency` and `permission-added-store-issue` — both hand-rolled double try/catch head-vs-base JSON parsing; now one `w.jsonPair(file)` returning `{ head, base }` with `null` for an absent/unparsable side.
- `client-id-single-origin` — `w.addedLines()` plus `w.filesContaining(literal)` replaced the inline read-and-scan.
- `squash-merge-history` and `task-lifecycle` — already near-minimal; wrapped for uniformity (`w.introducedMerges()`, `w.onDefaultBranch()`) and comment-trimmed.
- Left alone: `promote-scope` (a two-line path filter, nothing to absorb) and `warning-suppression` (check-the-world by design, out of this family).

**Faster**: `ctx.read`/`ctx.readBase` are now cached per sweep — checks are read-only over an immutable snapshot, several rules re-read the same files, and every `readBase` was a `git show` subprocess before.

**Trustworthy**: beyond the green suite, I mutation-tested the helper — weakening `Turns.last`, `danglingReferences`, and `jsonPair` each made existing fixtures bite (5, 2, and 6 failures), then restored byte-identical. I also cleaned up the three broken `.claude/skills/` symlinks (dangling leftovers of the pre-#385 layout) that the git stop-hook was complaining about.

> ✅ All done — helper landed, 8 rules rewritten, suite green, PR #393 open and ready for review. Say the word if you want me to watch the PR for CI/review activity, and "LGTM" when you want it merged.

attachment.content:
# Owner interaction preferences

Personal preferences from the repo owner for how the assistant should interact — tone, summary style, end-of-turn conventions, how results and decisions are surfaced, and the phrases the owner uses to trigger defined commands. These are **not** project conventions (those live in the [packs/](../packs/) corpus, each pack bundling its skills); they're how the owner likes to be worked with. Where a preference triggers a command whose mechanics are a project convention, the mechanics stay in their own doc and the entry here just owns the trigger phrase.

## Preferences

- **When there's nothing left to do, say so boldly at the end of the conversation.** Don't bury a "this is complete / I'm blocked / nothing further to do" behind hedging — make the terminal state unmistakable. **Set it apart visually so it catches the eye:** put the terminal state — or, when work remains, the list of open/outstanding items — in its own callout (a Markdown blockquote box, e.g. `> ✅ All done` or `> ⚠️ Still open: …`), not trailing prose the owner has to scan for. One or the other always gets the box: the done-signal when nothing remains, the open-items list when something does.
- **"LGTM"** is the owner's phrase for the merge-to-main command: it asks for the change in front of the owner to be merged into `main`. It's an owner-triggered command; the default mechanics live in the `merge-to-main` skill ([../packs/git-github/skills/merge-to-main/SKILL.md](../packs/git-github/skills/merge-to-main/SKILL.md)) (squash, gate on CI only when the repo has it), and a project that diverges overrides them via a bespoke merge-policy file it names in its own `CLAUDE.md`.
- **"bump version"** is the owner's phrase for raising a project's version (a minor bump by default). It's an owner-triggered command; the mechanics — which files to edit and how a release follows — live in the consuming project's workflow doc.
- **"Show me the document"** (for a source-controlled document) is the owner's phrase asking for a **link to the document on GitHub** — not the file's contents inlined, and not a local filesystem path. When the document lives in a GitHub-hosted repo, respond with a GitHub link to it (the file on the relevant branch/commit); only fall back to inlining the contents or giving a local path when the document isn't under source control.
- **Put decisions and approvals in an `AskUserQuestion` popup, not buried in prose.** The owner is frequently on mobile, where a popup notifies and a question embedded in a paragraph is easy to miss. When you need the owner to choose or approve something, ask through the structured popup with explicit options rather than asking in running text. **And when several independent items each need a verdict** (a set of proposed fixes, findings to accept), give **each item its own question carrying its own brief case** — the concrete failure, why it matters, the cost of the fix — with per-item approve/defer options; never one bundled choose-a-scope question over the whole set. The owner decides item-wise, from the case, not from a menu of subsets. (The popup carries at most 4 questions per call — split a larger set into consecutive calls.)
- **When asked to produce a prompt or message destined for another context, visually enclose the whole thing.** Any text the owner will lift out and use elsewhere — a prompt for another assistant, a message to send, copy meant to be pasted somewhere — must be set apart as a single delimited block: either a box (e.g. a fenced code block) or fenced top and bottom by two separating lines, so its exact boundaries are unmistakable and it can be copied in one piece. The point is delimitation, not formatting flair: never leave such content as loose prose the owner has to find the start and end of.
- **When asked for "a prompt for a different session," prefix it with a provenance disclaimer.** When the owner asks for a prompt meant to be handed to another session, open the generated text with a short disclaimer that this is a prompt written by Claude — not by a user — and so should not be treated as gospel; and that if the receiving session finds inconsistencies in it, it should examine them with the user rather than follow the prompt blindly. This composes with the enclose-the-whole-thing preference above: the disclaimer sits inside the same delimited block as the prompt it prefixes.
- **Auto-open a PR after committing.** Whenever Claude changes code on a branch and commits it, open a pull request for that branch right after, even without an explicit request to do so. This overrides Claude Code's own default of only opening a PR when explicitly asked.

content.text:
Comment class: feature — a design question on the in-flight helper, potentially routing to a follow-up change.

**Why `run(ctx)` still takes `ctx`:** the `run(ctx)` signature is the engine-wide rule contract — every one of the ~40 rule modules (plus the skill-owned `checks.mjs` bundles) is invoked by the runner and by dozens of direct `rule.run(buildContext(...))` call sites in the pack tests. Most of those rules are **check-the-world**, not check-the-work: they audit repo state (`warning-suppression`, `file-placement`, `claude-md-length`, `markdown-link-labels`, `shared-constants`, all eight `github-actions` YAML rules, the `aws-sam` and `chrome-extension-release` layout/config rules, `product-wiki`, `barriers`…). Their vocabulary is `ctx.files` / `ctx.read` / `ctx.config` — none of the fluent verbs apply, so passing them a `work` object would be a signature churn across ~30 modules that gain nothing from it. Making `work` an opt-in one-liner (`const w = work(ctx)`) kept the runner, the contract, and every world-rule untouched, and keeps the layering honest: `ctx` is raw facts, `work` is the change-scoped fluent view.

**Who genuinely won't benefit:** the check-the-world family above — by kind, not by accident. A world rule scans everything as it exists now; it has no "the work" to be fluent about. The only gray zone is four small `addedLines` consumers (`no-enforcement-narration`, `email-verified`, `token-audience`, `recognition-feature-detect`) — they're work-scoped but already one line via the `line-scanning` import, so switching them to `w.addedLines()` is uniformity, not savings.

That said, there is a middle path that gets you the ergonomics you're pointing at without breaking the contract: fold the fluent methods **into the context itself** (built once in `buildContext`), so every rule keeps `run(ctx)` but writes `ctx.ownerTurns().last()` / `ctx.deadLinks()` directly — no wrapper line at all, and world rules simply never call the extra methods. The cost is one grab-bag object instead of two layers, and a naming decision around `conversation()` (today it returns raw entries, which `capture-log`/`render-dialogue` consume).

questions.question: How should rules receive the fluent check-the-work API?

options.label: Keep work(ctx) wrapper (Recommended)
options.description: Status quo on PR #393: ctx stays raw facts, work(ctx) is an opt-in fluent view. Zero churn on the ~30 check-the-world rules and the runner; costs one line in each work-scoped rule.

options.label: Fold fluent methods into ctx
options.description: buildContext gains ownerTurns()/deadLinks()/jsonPair()/etc., rules call them directly with no wrapper line. Touches the context builder + 8 rules; one flatter object, keeps run(ctx) everywhere.

options.label: Change signature to run(work)
options.description: work delegates every ctx field, runner passes it to all rules. Fully uniform but churns every rule module, the runner, and dozens of test call sites for no gain in the world rules.

input.body:
Closes #392.

## What

Adds `engine/checks/helpers/work.mjs` — a fluent, null-safe check-the-work context — and makes the check-the-work rules **receive it as their `run` argument**: a rule declares `scope: 'work'` and its signature becomes `run(w)`, so the scope split (judge the change vs. audit the repo) is visible in the rule itself. Check-the-world rules keep the raw `ctx` until a fluent world object lands (future change, per owner direction).

`runRule(rule, ctx, ...args)` is the single dispatch seam — the runner and every rule test invoke rules through it, so a rule's scope decides its context in exactly one place.

The fluent API (mechanism only; a rule still owns its patterns, file filters, and failure text):

- `w.conversation().ownerTurns().last()` — transcript chain that never needs a guard ladder: no transcript ⇒ empty turns; `.last()` on empty ⇒ a null-object turn whose predicates (`classified()`, `classes()`, `reply()`, `time()`, `excerpt(n)`) all answer emptily. `Turns` is an Array subclass, so `.filter(...)` stays chainable and keeps `.last()`.
- `w.jsonPair(file)` — `{ head, base }` parsed JSON at head and the scoping base; absent/unparsable side ⇒ `null`.
- `w.deadLinks()` / `w.danglingReferences(tolerated)` — the two reference-integrity walks (relative-link resolution; deleted-path mentions with the widen-to-path-token innocence test).
- `w.addedLines()`, `w.branchCommits()` (with parsed `.time`), `w.introducedMerges()`, `w.onDefaultBranch()`, `w.filesContaining(needle)`.
- Delegated raw surface (`files`, `changedFiles`, `tracked`, `read`, `exists`, `branch`, `baseRef`, `commits`, `packConfig(id)`) so a work rule — and structural helpers like `findExtensionManifest` — never touches `ctx`.

Work rules converted (`scope: 'work'`, `run(w)`): `comment-classification`, `squash-merge-history`, `reference-integrity`, `task-lifecycle`, `feature-requirements-first`, `node/earn-each-dependency`, `cer/permission-added-store-issue`, `google-client-id-single-origin`. Preamble comments trimmed to the load-bearing whys.

Deliberately **not** converted: the `matchingLines`-based skill rules (`email-verified`, `token-audience`, `recognition-feature-detect`, `no-enforcement-narration`) — they are check-the-world by design ("repo-state on purpose"), and will get the world object later.

## Performance

`ctx.read` and `ctx.readBase` are now cached for the sweep — checks are read-only over an immutable snapshot, many rules re-read the same files, and each `readBase` call was a `git show` subprocess.

## Verification

- Full CI suite (489 tests) green before and after each step — the rules' fixture tests pin behavior, so the refactor is proven behavior-preserving.
- See-it-fail by mutation: weakening `Turns.last` (5 failures), `danglingReferences` (2), `jsonPair`'s base side (6), and the `runRule` scope dispatch itself (18) each made existing fixtures bite; restored byte-identical afterwards.
- End-to-end sweep (`node engine/checks/check_the_world.mjs`) runs clean: 0 blocking.

Comment class: feature.

Done — your answer, then what I changed on the back of it.

**Why `run` wasn't taking `work`:** `run(ctx)` is the engine-wide rule contract shared with the ~30 check-the-world rules, whose vocabulary is `ctx.files`/`ctx.read` — I'd kept `work` as an opt-in wrapper to avoid churning them. Per your direction, that's now flipped for the work family: a rule declares `scope: 'work'` and its signature becomes `run(w)` — the scope split is visible in the rule itself. `runRule(rule, ctx)` in `work.mjs` is the single dispatch seam (used by the runner and every rule test), and the residual `ctx` pokes folded into `work` as you hinted: `w.branch`, `w.commits`, `w.baseRef`, `w.changedFiles`, `w.packConfig(id)`, `w.read`/`w.exists` — so a work rule (and structural helpers like `findExtensionManifest`, which now receives `w`) never touches `ctx` at all. World rules keep raw `ctx` untouched, awaiting the future `world` object.

**Rules that won't benefit** — and it's by kind, not oversight: `email-verified`, `token-audience`, `recognition-feature-detect`, and `no-enforcement-narration` looked work-adjacent (they import from `line-scanning`), but reading them settled it — each is explicitly "repo-state on purpose" (a pre-existing auth bypass must keep firing however long ago it merged), scanning all of `ctx.files` with `matchingLines`. They're world rules and stay on `ctx` for your future world-object change, alongside the rest of the world family (`warning-suppression`, `file-placement`, the YAML/layout rules…). `capture-log`/`render-dialogue` turned out to be CLI scripts, not rules. So the work family is exactly the 8 converted rules.

Verified: full suite green (489), mutation on the dispatch itself bites (18 failures when `runRule` hands work rules raw `ctx`, proving tests exercise the seam), and the end-to-end sweep runs 0 blocking. Pushed as a second commit on [PR #393](https://github.com/missingbulb/Claudinite/pull/393), description updated.

> ✅ All done — scope-dispatched `run(w)` landed for all 8 work rules, world rules deliberately deferred to the future `world` object. PR #393 ready for review; "LGTM" when you want it merged.

# Owner interaction preferences

Personal preferences from the repo owner for how the assistant should interact — tone, summary style, end-of-turn conventions, how results and decisions are surfaced, and the phrases the owner uses to trigger defined commands. These are **not** project conventions (those live in the [packs/](../packs/) corpus, each pack bundling its skills); they're how the owner likes to be worked with. Where a preference triggers a command whose mechanics are a project convention, the mechanics stay in their own doc and the entry here just owns the trigger phrase.

## Preferences

- **When there's nothing left to do, say so boldly at the end of the conversation.** Don't bury a "this is complete / I'm blocked / nothing further to do" behind hedging — make the terminal state unmistakable. **Set it apart visually so it catches the eye:** put the terminal state — or, when work remains, the list of open/outstanding items — in its own callout (a Markdown blockquote box, e.g. `> ✅ All done` or `> ⚠️ Still open: …`), not trailing prose the owner has to scan for. One or the other always gets the box: the done-signal when nothing remains, the open-items list when something does.
- **"LGTM"** is the owner's phrase for the merge-to-main command: it asks for the change in front of the owner to be merged into `main`. It's an owner-triggered command; the default mechanics live in the `merge-to-main` skill ([../packs/git-github/skills/merge-to-main/SKILL.md](../packs/git-github/skills/merge-to-main/SKILL.md)) (squash, gate on CI only when the repo has it), and a project that diverges overrides them via a bespoke merge-policy file it names in its own `CLAUDE.md`.
- **"bump version"** is the owner's phrase for raising a project's version (a minor bump by default). It's an owner-triggered command; the mechanics — which files to edit and how a release follows — live in the consuming project's workflow doc.
- **"Show me the document"** (for a source-controlled document) is the owner's phrase asking for a **link to the document on GitHub** — not the file's contents inlined, and not a local filesystem path. When the document lives in a GitHub-hosted repo, respond with a GitHub link to it (the file on the relevant branch/commit); only fall back to inlining the contents or giving a local path when the document isn't under source control.
- **Put decisions and approvals in an `AskUserQuestion` popup, not buried in prose.** The owner is frequently on mobile, where a popup notifies and a question embedded in a paragraph is easy to miss. When you need the owner to choose or approve something, ask through the structured popup with explicit options rather than asking in running text. **And when several independent items each need a verdict** (a set of proposed fixes, findings to accept), give **each item its own question carrying its own brief case** — the concrete failure, why it matters, the cost of the fix — with per-item approve/defer options; never one bundled choose-a-scope question over the whole set. The owner decides item-wise, from the case, not from a menu of subsets. (The popup carries at most 4 questions per call — split a larger set into consecutive calls.)
- **When asked to produce a prompt or message destined for another context, visually enclose the whole thing.** Any text the owner will lift out and use elsewhere — a prompt for another assistant, a message to send, copy meant to be pasted somewhere — must be set apart as a single delimited block: either a box (e.g. a fenced code block) or fenced top and bottom by two separating lines, so its exact boundaries are unmistakable and it can be copied in one piece. The point is delimitation, not formatting flair: never leave such content as loose prose the owner has to find the start and end of.
- **When asked for "a prompt for a different session," prefix it with a provenance disclaimer.** When the owner asks for a prompt meant to be handed to another session, open the generated text with a short disclaimer that this is a prompt written by Claude — not by a user — and so should not be treated as gospel; and that if the receiving session finds inconsistencies in it, it should examine them with the user rather than follow the prompt blindly. This composes with the enclose-the-whole-thing preference above: the disclaimer sits inside the same delimited block as the prompt it prefixes.
- **Auto-open a PR after committing.** Whenever Claude changes code on a branch and commits it, open a pull request for that branch right after, even without an explicit request to do so. This overrides Claude Code's own default of only opening a PR when explicitly asked.

1	import { finding } from '../../engine/checks/helpers/findings.mjs';
2	
3	// The effect check behind the squash-only platform setting, scoped to the work:
4	// pre-existing merges already on the base are out of range.
5	const rule = {
6	  id: 'squash-merge-history',
7	  severity: 'blocking',
8	  description: 'A change introduces no merge commits (it lands squashed, not merged)',
9	  doc: 'skills/merge-to-main/SKILL.md',
10	  scope: 'work',
11	  why: 'the squash-only repo setting can be off or bypassed; a merge commit in the work is the effect that proves it',
12	
13	  run(w) {
14	    return w.introducedMerges().map(({ sha, subject }) =>
15	      finding(rule, {
16	        file: `${w.branch || 'HEAD'}@${sha}`,
17	        what: `merge commit introduced by this change: ${subject}`,
18	        fix: 'rebase to drop the merge commit (git pull --rebase, or git rebase onto the base) so the branch lands squashed; keep squash-only enabled in the repo settings, or turn the rule off if the project deliberately runs a non-squash policy named in its CLAUDE.md',
19	      })
20	    );
21	  },
22	};
23	
24	export default rule;

questions.question: How should the runner structure be fixed so "check the world" stops meaning "run everything"?

options.label: Rename to neutral sweep (Recommended)
options.description: The one-pass both-scopes sweep is correct (Stop is the single enforcement point and must see both); only the names lie. Rename check_the_world.mjs → a neutral runner (e.g. run_checks.mjs) and check_the_work.mjs → the stop-gate name it really is, leaving thin legacy shims at the old paths so consumer CI backstops and docs keep working until a migration retires them.

options.label: True scope-split entries
options.description: Extract the sweep machinery into a shared module; check_the_world runs only world rules, check_the_work runs only work rules, and Stop/CI invoke both. Architecturally purest, but today every real caller needs both scopes anyway, so it doubles entry points and process spawns for no behavioral gain.

options.label: Defer to the world-object change
options.description: Leave the runner as-is now; fold the rename/split into the planned future change that gives world rules their own fluent object, so the structure moves once instead of twice. The misleading names live on until then.

cd /home/user/Claudinite; python3 - <<'EOF'
import pathlib, re

# Rename run(w) → run(work) and w.* → work.* in the eight work rules; plus the
# two local-variable names the owner called out.
rules = [
  'packs/basics/comment-classification.mjs',
  'packs/basics/squash-merge-history.mjs',
  'packs/basics/reference-integrity.mjs',
  'packs/basics/task-lifecycle.mjs',
  'packs/executable-requirements/feature-requirements-first.mjs',
  'packs/node/earn-each-dependency.mjs',
  'packs/chrome-extension-release/permission-added-store-issue.mjs',
  'packs/google-identity/skills/google-id-token-validation/client-id-single-origin.mjs',
]
for path in rules:
    p = pathlib.Path(path)
    t = p.read_text()
    t = t.replace('run(w) {', 'run(work) {')
    t = re.sub(r'\bw\.', 'work.', t)
    t = t.replace('(w) => (gone)', '(work) => (gone)')
    t = t.replace('specPath(w)', 'specPath(work)')
    t = t.replace('function specPath(w)'.replace('w','work'), 'function specPath(work)')  # no-op guard
    p.write_text(t)

# comment-classification: last → lastOwnerTurn
p = pathlib.Path('packs/basics/comment-classification.mjs'); t = p.read_text()
t = t.replace('const last = work.conversation().ownerTurns().last();', 'const lastOwnerTurn = work.conversation().ownerTurns().last();')
t = t.replace('if (!last.exists || last.classified()) return [];', 'if (!lastOwnerTurn.exists || lastOwnerTurn.classified()) return [];')
t = t.replace('${last.excerpt(70)}', '${lastOwnerTurn.excerpt(70)}')
p.write_text(t)

# feature-requirements-first: feature → lastFeatureTurn, same spirit
p = pathlib.Path('packs/executable-requirements/feature-requirements-first.mjs'); t = p.read_text()
t = t.replace("const feature = work.conversation().ownerTurns().filter((t) => t.classes().has('feature')).last();",
              "const lastFeatureTurn = work.conversation().ownerTurns().filter((t) => t.classes().has('feature')).last();")
t = t.replace('if (!feature.exists) return [];', 'if (!lastFeatureTurn.exists) return [];')
t = t.replace('c.time >= feature.time()', 'c.time >= lastFeatureTurn.time()')
p.write_text(t)
print('renamed')
EOF
grep -rn "run(w)\|\bw\." packs/basics/comment-classification.mjs packs/basics/reference-integrity.mjs packs/executable-requirements/feature-requirements-first.mjs | grep -v "work\." || echo "no stray w left"
node --check packs/basics/reference-integrity.mjs && node --check packs/executable-requirements/feature-requirements-first.mjs && echo "syntax ok"

## Part 8 — land the adoption green

Run the sweep once and clear what it surfaces:

```sh
node .claudinite/shared/engine/checks/check_the_world.mjs
```

On a repo with existing code, **expect a backlog** — enforcement scope is whole-repo, and
findings in code you never touched would otherwise block every future session's Stop hook and

  packs sit in its `.claudinite/local_packs/<name>/`, same slots, same engine.

session still sees every finding; CI runs both too. Also rename the work
rules' run argument w -> work and the transcript locals to

input.body:
Closes #392.

## What

Two moves, building on each other:

**1. Fluent `work` context.** `engine/checks/helpers/work.mjs` is a null-safe, fluent view over the raw `ctx`. A rule that judges the current change declares `scope: 'work'` and its `run(work)` receives this object — so guard ladders collapse into the chain (`work.conversation().ownerTurns().last()`), head-vs-base JSON is one `work.jsonPair(file)`, and the two reference-integrity walks are `work.deadLinks()` / `work.danglingReferences(tolerated)`. `Turns` is an Array subclass, so `.filter(...)` stays chainable and `.last()` returns a null-object turn (no existence guard needed). `work` delegates the raw surface a work rule still needs (`files`, `read`, `exists`, `branch`, `commits`, `packConfig(id)`), so a work rule — and structural helpers like `findExtensionManifest` — never touches `ctx`.

**2. Split the runner by scope.** The single runner ran every rule regardless of scope, so "check the world" meant "run everything" and the work rules had no home. The sweep pipeline is now `engine/checks/sweep.mjs`, with a thin entry per scope:

- `check_the_world.mjs` — repo-state rules + the settings/interview diagnostics (themselves repo state), and `--list` / `--init`.
- `check_the_work.mjs` — the `scope: 'work'` rules, each dispatched through the fluent `work` view, `--transcript` enabling the conversation rules.

A rule's scope decides which sweep runs it, in one place (`runRule` in `work.mjs`; `inScope` in `sweep.mjs`). The Stop gate moved into `engine/hooks/stop-command.mjs`: it fast-exits when nothing changed, then runs **both** runners (work with the transcript) so the session still sees every finding. CI runs both too.

Work rules converted (`scope: 'work'`, `run(work)`): `comment-classification`, `squash-merge-history`, `reference-integrity`, `task-lifecycle`, `feature-requirements-first`, `node/earn-each-dependency`, `cer/permission-added-store-issue`, `google-client-id-single-origin`. Transcript locals renamed to `lastOwnerTurn` / `lastFeatureTurn`; preamble comments trimmed to the load-bearing whys.

Deliberately **not** converted: the `matchingLines`-based skill rules (`email-verified`, `token-audience`, `recognition-feature-detect`, `no-enforcement-narration`) — check-the-world by design ("repo-state on purpose"); they get the world object in a future change.

## Performance

`ctx.read` / `ctx.readBase` are cached for the sweep — checks are read-only over an immutable snapshot, many rules re-read the same files, and each `readBase` was a `git show` subprocess.

## Verification

- Full CI suite (489 tests) green at every step.
- Scope separation proven end-to-end on a fixture carrying both a world violation (`warning-suppression`) and work violations (`reference-integrity`, `task-lifecycle`): the world runner reports only the former, the work runner only the latter, and the Stop gate reports all three at exit 2.
- See-it-fail by mutation: weakening `Turns.last`, `danglingReferences`, `jsonPair`'s base side, and the `runRule` scope dispatch each made existing fixtures bite; restored byte-identical.
- Re-homed the barriers interview exception from `check_the_world.mjs` onto `sweep.mjs` (where the `interview.mjs` coupling now lives); both scope sweeps run 0 blocking on the repo.

Also removed three broken `.claude/skills/` symlinks left dangling by the pre-#385 top-level `skills/` layout.

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)

https://claude.ai/code/session_015EGXi3y8hZNfS7A9csREv1

content.text: {"id":"4107354760","url":"https://github.com/missingbulb/Claudinite/pull/393"}

content: You mentioned "double process executions". Why is that? At the time we run cehck-the-work tests - we don't need to run check-the-world. Those are different timings. When Claud uses a skill and finished the work - the "check the work" tests run. When it finished a task (and ready to commit to branch) - check the world runs. Different timing.

content.text:
Comment class: correction (a design flaw you caught) plus feature (the naming fixes).

You were right — `check_the_world.mjs` had no business running work rules, and the name was lying. The single runner ran *every* rule regardless of scope; "world" had quietly come to mean "everything," which is exactly why the odd `import { runRule } from './work.mjs'` sat at the top of a file that shouldn't touch work at all.

**The split** (your chosen option — true scope-split entries):
- `engine/checks/sweep.mjs` — the shared pipeline (build context → activate packs → run one scope's rule family → apply overrides → render), so neither entry duplicates it.
- `check_the_world.mjs` — world rules only (repo-state), plus `--list`/`--init` and the settings/interview diagnostics, which *are* repo state.
- `check_the_work.mjs` — `scope: 'work'` rules only, each dispatched through the fluent view, `--transcript` feeding the conversation rules.
- The Stop gate moved into `engine/hooks/stop-command.mjs`: it fast-exits when nothing changed, then runs **both** runners so a session still sees every finding. CI runs both too.

Proven end-to-end on a fixture with one world violation (`warning-suppression`) and two work violations (`reference-integrity`, `task-lifecycle`): world reports only the world one, work reports only the work ones, the gate reports all three at exit 2. That separation *is* the see-it-fail evidence — before the split, all three came out of one runner.

**Naming**, per your two calls: `run(w)` → `run(work)` everywhere (the over-abbreviation), and `const last` → `const lastOwnerTurn` in comment-classification (plus its sibling `lastFeatureTurn` in feature-requirements-first, same spirit).

One fallout I had to chase: extracting the interview import into `sweep.mjs` moved the core→pack coupling, so the barriers `except` entry that excused it had to move onto `sweep.mjs` too — the world sweep caught it as a stale exception, which is the barrier check doing its job. Both sweeps now run 0 blocking. Full suite 489/489. Pushed as a third commit on [PR #393](https://github.com/missingbulb/Claudinite/pull/393).

> ✅ All done — runner split by scope, `check_the_world` no longer imports or runs any work rule, names fixed, both sweeps green, suite 489/489. PR #393 updated and ready for review; "LGTM" to merge.

Want me to watch #393 for CI and review comments (I can autofix failures and reply to reviews via the PR-activity subscription), or leave it?

lastPrompt: You mentioned "double process executions". Why is that? At the time we run cehck-the-work tests - we don't need to run check-the-world. Those are different timings. When Claud uses a skill and finishe…

content.text:
You're right, and it's my design flaw, not just loose wording. Here's the honest answer to "why double execution":

In the PR I listed "doubles process spawns" as a *con* of splitting the runner — then in `stop-command.mjs` I made the Stop gate spawn **both** runners on every turn anyway. That's because I conflated two different things: *the scopes are separate code* (correct) with *the scopes fire at the same moment* (wrong). I wired both to the Stop event, so every turn pays for a whole-repo world sweep it doesn't need. Your point dissolves the "con" entirely: if the two scopes fire at different times, there's no doubling — each runs once, when it's meaningful.

Your timing model maps cleanly onto the hooks that already exist:

- **check-the-work → the Stop hook** (already wired). It fires at the end of each work turn — exactly "Claude finished a chunk of work." It's the per-change feedback loop, and `comment-classification` *has* to live here anyway (it judges the reply to the latest comment, which only exists at Stop). Cheap, scoped to the diff + transcript.
- **check-the-world → commit time.** The whole-repo sweep is only *meaningful* at a commit boundary — "ready to record this to the branch." Running it every turn is the waste you're pointing at. The natural home is the **PreToolUse Bash guard that's already wired** (`pretooluse-command.mjs` — today it only blocks remote-branch-deletes): intercept `git commit`, run the world sweep, exit 2 to block the commit while blocking findings remain.

So Stop spawns one runner (work), a commit spawns the other (world) — the double-spawn is gone, and each scope fires on its real trigger.

The one thing that makes this a decision rather than a mechanical fix: moving world *off* Stop touches a prior owner call. #385 explicitly kept the whole-repo sweep on Stop as the backstop for edits made **outside** Claude sessions ("no CI job… edits surface at the next session's Stop sweep"). If world only runs at Claude-driven commits, that backstop weakens — an external edit surfaces at the next *commit Claude makes*, not the next Stop. That's a real tradeoff, so I want your call on the world trigger before I rewire the (deliberately stable) hook contract.

Consistent with Claudinite's settings.json wiring, committed in-tree. Covers Claude-driven commits; a human/external commit surfaces at Claude's next commit, not immediately.

questions.question: check-the-work stays on Stop (per turn). Where should check-the-world fire, now that it leaves Stop?

It should run at a similar time when you choose to run the project tests. What's the trigger for that? Or to think about it differently - perhaps we can wire it to the project tests and be spared the decision?

content.text:
Comment class: feature — refining where the world scope is triggered.

Direct answer to "what's the trigger for running the project tests": **there isn't a hook for it.** Claude Code fires hooks on `SessionStart`, `PreToolUse`/`PostToolUse`, `Stop`, `SubagentStop` — but nothing fires "the tests are running." Locally, I run the suite by *judgment* (to verify a change before committing or before finishing a turn); the only *deterministic* trigger is CI, on push/PR. So there's no test-run event to hook `check_the_world` onto the way there is for Stop — which is exactly why your reframe is the better path.

**Your instinct is right: don't give `check_the_world` a hook at all — make it a test.** A whole-repo conformance sweep asserting "every invariant still holds across the repo" *is* a test — same shape as any other suite assertion. Wire it in and it runs wherever the suite runs, and the entire trigger decision (PreToolUse-git-commit vs git-pre-commit-hook — both options from the last popup) simply disappears. You're spared the decision because there's no longer a decision to make.

Concretely for this repo:
- Add `engine-tests/conformance-world.test.mjs` that spawns `check_the_world.mjs` over the repo and asserts zero blocking findings. Now `node --test` runs the world sweep — locally when I run the tests to verify, and in CI.
- Drop the separate "Conformance sweep" world step from `ci.yml` (redundant once it's a test).
- Remove `check_the_world` from the Stop hook — **Stop runs `check_the_work` only** (per-turn feedback; it needs the live transcript and gives per-change signal, so it stays a hook, not a test).

The result is exactly your timing: work checks after each unit of work (Stop), world checks when the tests run — and no double spawn, because neither scope fires on the other's trigger.

One caveat I owe you, since it's the flip side of #385: a *consumer* repo with no test suite and no CI has no test-run moment either — so its only world backstop today is precisely the Stop sweep we'd be removing. For **this** (canon) repo the change is clean; for the barebones-consumer case it reopens "what catches external edits there." I'd keep this change scoped to the canon repo's wiring and treat the consumer default as a separate question rather than silently changing every consumer's backstop.

I recommend proceeding with world-as-a-test for the canon repo as described. Want me to implement it — and should I hold the consumer-side Stop backstop question for a separate pass, or fold a decision on it in now?

attachment.content:
# Owner interaction preferences

Personal preferences from the repo owner for how the assistant should interact — tone, summary style, end-of-turn conventions, how results and decisions are surfaced, and the phrases the owner uses to trigger defined commands. These are **not** project conventions (those live in the [packs/](../packs/) corpus, each pack bundling its skills); they're how the owner likes to be worked with. Where a preference triggers a command whose mechanics are a project convention, the mechanics stay in their own doc and the entry here just owns the trigger phrase.

## Preferences

- **When there's nothing left to do, say so boldly at the end of the conversation.** Don't bury a "this is complete / I'm blocked / nothing further to do" behind hedging — make the terminal state unmistakable. **Set it apart visually so it catches the eye:** put the terminal state — or, when work remains, the list of open/outstanding items — in its own callout (a Markdown blockquote box, e.g. `> ✅ All done` or `> ⚠️ Still open: …`), not trailing prose the owner has to scan for. One or the other always gets the box: the done-signal when nothing remains, the open-items list when something does.
- **"LGTM"** is the owner's phrase for the merge-to-main command: it asks for the change in front of the owner to be merged into `main`. It's an owner-triggered command; the default mechanics live in the `merge-to-main` skill ([../packs/git-github/skills/merge-to-main/SKILL.md](../packs/git-github/skills/merge-to-main/SKILL.md)) (squash, gate on CI only when the repo has it), and a project that diverges overrides them via a bespoke merge-policy file it names in its own `CLAUDE.md`.
- **"bump version"** is the owner's phrase for raising a project's version (a minor bump by default). It's an owner-triggered command; the mechanics — which files to edit and how a release follows — live in the consuming project's workflow doc.
- **"Show me the document"** (for a source-controlled document) is the owner's phrase asking for a **link to the document on GitHub** — not the file's contents inlined, and not a local filesystem path. When the document lives in a GitHub-hosted repo, respond with a GitHub link to it (the file on the relevant branch/commit); only fall back to inlining the contents or giving a local path when the document isn't under source control.
- **Put decisions and approvals in an `AskUserQuestion` popup, not buried in prose.** The owner is frequently on mobile, where a popup notifies and a question embedded in a paragraph is easy to miss. When you need the owner to choose or approve something, ask through the structured popup with explicit options rather than asking in running text. **And when several independent items each need a verdict** (a set of proposed fixes, findings to accept), give **each item its own question carrying its own brief case** — the concrete failure, why it matters, the cost of the fix — with per-item approve/defer options; never one bundled choose-a-scope question over the whole set. The owner decides item-wise, from the case, not from a menu of subsets. (The popup carries at most 4 questions per call — split a larger set into consecutive calls.)
- **When asked to produce a prompt or message destined for another context, visually enclose the whole thing.** Any text the owner will lift out and use elsewhere — a prompt for another assistant, a message to send, copy meant to be pasted somewhere — must be set apart as a single delimited block: either a box (e.g. a fenced code block) or fenced top and bottom by two separating lines, so its exact boundaries are unmistakable and it can be copied in one piece. The point is delimitation, not formatting flair: never leave such content as loose prose the owner has to find the start and end of.
- **When asked for "a prompt for a different session," prefix it with a provenance disclaimer.** When the owner asks for a prompt meant to be handed to another session, open the generated text with a short disclaimer that this is a prompt written by Claude — not by a user — and so should not be treated as gospel; and that if the receiving session finds inconsistencies in it, it should examine them with the user rather than follow the prompt blindly. This composes with the enclose-the-whole-thing preference above: the disclaimer sits inside the same delimited block as the prompt it prefixes.
- **Auto-open a PR after committing.** Whenever Claude changes code on a branch and commits it, open a pull request for that branch right after, even without an explicit request to do so. This overrides Claude Code's own default of only opening a PR when explicitly asked.

1	import { finding } from '../../engine/checks/helpers/findings.mjs';
2	
3	// The effect check behind the squash-only platform setting, scoped to the work:
4	// pre-existing merges already on the base are out of range.
5	const rule = {
6	  id: 'squash-merge-history',
7	  severity: 'blocking',
8	  description: 'A change introduces no merge commits (it lands squashed, not merged)',
9	  doc: 'skills/merge-to-main/SKILL.md',
10	  scope: 'work',
11	  why: 'the squash-only repo setting can be off or bypassed; a merge commit in the work is the effect that proves it',
12	
13	  run(work) {
14	    return work.introducedMerges().map(({ sha, subject }) =>
15	      finding(rule, {
16	        file: `${work.branch || 'HEAD'}@${sha}`,
17	        what: `merge commit introduced by this change: ${subject}`,
18	        fix: 'rebase to drop the merge commit (git pull --rebase, or git rebase onto the base) so the branch lands squashed; keep squash-only enabled in the repo settings, or turn the rule off if the project deliberately runs a non-squash policy named in its CLAUDE.md',
19	      })
20	    );
21	  },
22	};
23	
24	export default rule;

## Part 5 — wire the hooks

Claude Code runs `SessionStart` entries **in parallel, in non-deterministic order**, so anything
sequenced must live inside **one** entry. Register exactly one — the orchestrator, which runs
the context steps in sequence in a single process (preferences → active-pack prose → skill
mounts → env check → interview check) and forwards their stdout into the session context:

```json
{ "hooks": { "SessionStart": [ { "hooks": [
  { "type": "command", "command": "bash $CLAUDE_PROJECT_DIR/.claudinite/shared/engine/hooks/session-start-command.sh" }
] } ] } }
```

Register the Stop hook (runs the conformance sweep when the session changed something; blocks
the stop while blocking findings remain) and the PreToolUse guard (deterministically blocks
forbidden commands) alongside it:

```json
{ "hooks": { "Stop": [ { "hooks": [
  { "type": "command", "command": "node $CLAUDE_PROJECT_DIR/.claudinite/shared/engine/hooks/stop-command.mjs" }
] } ],
  "PreToolUse": [ { "matcher": "Bash", "hooks": [
  { "type": "command", "command": "node $CLAUDE_PROJECT_DIR/.claudinite/shared/engine/hooks/pretooluse-command.mjs" }
] } ] } }
```

Invoke scripts **through `bash`/`node`**, never as bare paths — a dropped exec bit would fail the
hook before line 1 and swallow its own message. Notes on how the steps behave:

- **Preferences are fail-soft** — per-user content is never vendored;
  `shared/engine/hooks/inject-preferences.sh` reads a local copy where the tree has one and otherwise
  fetches the single `preferences/<email>.md`; any miss is a one-line note, and the session

the context steps in sequence in a single process (preferences → active-pack prose → skill
mounts → env check → interview check) and forwards their stdout into the session context:

```json
{ "hooks": { "SessionStart": [ { "hooks": [
  { "type": "command", "command": "bash $CLAUDE_PROJECT_DIR/.claudinite/shared/engine/hooks/session-start-command.sh" }
] } ] } }
```

Register the Stop hook (runs the conformance sweep when the session changed something; blocks
the stop while blocking findings remain) and the PreToolUse guard (deterministically blocks
forbidden commands) alongside it:

```json
{ "hooks": { "Stop": [ { "hooks": [
  { "type": "command", "command": "node $CLAUDE_PROJECT_DIR/.claudinite/shared/engine/hooks/stop-command.mjs" }
] } ],
  "PreToolUse": [ { "matcher": "Bash", "hooks": [
  { "type": "command", "command": "node $CLAUDE_PROJECT_DIR/.claudinite/shared/engine/hooks/pretooluse-command.mjs" }
] } ] } }
```

Invoke scripts **through `bash`/`node`**, never as bare paths — a dropped exec bit would fail the
hook before line 1 and swallow its own message. Notes on how the steps behave:

- **Preferences are fail-soft** — per-user content is never vendored;
  `shared/engine/hooks/inject-preferences.sh` reads a local copy where the tree has one and otherwise
  fetches the single `preferences/<email>.md`; any miss is a one-line note, and the session

content.text:
Grounding done. Here's where things stand and the plan.

**Current committed state:** the scope split is in (`41df887`). Stop runs **both** runners — the double-spawn you flagged. Canon `ci.yml` already runs `check_the_world` + `check_the_work` as a standalone step (lines 34–39). The thing your directive changes is the *contract*, recorded at `engine/checks/README.md:134` as #385: **"No CI job in the standard wiring — edits outside Claude sessions surface at the next session's Stop sweep."** That decision is precisely *why* world lived on Stop — so consumers needn't set up CI.

**Your two directives resolve to:**
- World's home becomes the **project's test/CI flow**, invoked as a standalone `node …/check_the_world.mjs` **step** — never a `*.test.mjs` a Node runner discovers, since a Python/Rust/Go consumer's suite can't load it. It's the engine's own Node CLI, called as one step in whatever aggregates that project's tests (a CI job, a `make test`, an npm script).
- **Bootstrap** (bootstrap.md + the adopt-claudinite skill) must wire that step in during adoption.
- **Stop → `check_the_work` only** — the per-turn feedback loop, which needs the live transcript.

That's all clearly directed and I'll implement it in one pass. The **one genuine fork** I won't decide for you — because getting it wrong silently drops enforcement — is the flip side of #385: a consumer with **no** test/CI flow has nowhere for world to run once it leaves Stop.

questions.question: Once world moves off Stop into the test/CI flow, a consumer with NO test/CI flow loses its only world backstop (the #385 Stop sweep). How should bootstrap handle that case?

options.label: Keep world on Stop as a no-CI fallback (Recommended)
options.description: Bootstrap wires world into the test/CI flow when one exists (an explicit materialized config knob, e.g. worldTrigger: 'ci'); where none does, world stays on the Stop hook so enforcement never silently drops. The canon repo (has CI) sets worldTrigger:'ci' and takes world off Stop. Preserves #385 exactly where it's still needed; Stop behavior is config-driven, not hardcoded.

options.label: World always leaves Stop; bootstrap adds a minimal flow
options.description: Every consumer runs world in a test/CI step; a repo with no CI gets a minimal one wired by bootstrap. Uniform Stop behavior everywhere (work-only), but forces a CI/test-runner setup onto repos that deliberately had none — a full reversal of #385.

options.label: World always leaves Stop; no fallback
options.description: World runs only where a test/CI flow is wired. A bare repo with none has no automatic world enforcement until its owner wires a step. Simplest and most uniform, but silently drops the #385 backstop for those repos.

216	## Enforcement wiring
217	
218	**A Stop hook is the guarantee; instructions are not.** Wiring the checks into `npm test` and
219	hoping the agent runs it is exactly the instruction-following this design escapes. Instead,
220	bootstrap registers one more hook in the consumer's `.claude/settings.json` (the same mechanism
221	as the existing SessionStart hooks):
222	
223	1. The Stop hook fires when the agent finishes a turn.
224	2. It **fast-exits in milliseconds** when no tracked file differs from `main` — conversational
225	   turns cost nothing.
226	3. Otherwise it runs the full sweep; on findings it exits 2 with the findings on stderr.
227	   Claude Code blocks the stop and feeds that text back to the agent, which fixes the
228	   violations **in the same session**. A clean run stops silently.
229	
230	Loop safety comes from convergence (fixed findings stop firing) plus Claude Code's own runaway
231	protection (it overrides a Stop hook after ~8 consecutive blocks).
232	
233	**The Stop hook is the whole enforcement surface — consumers ship no CI job.** Hooks fire only
234	in Claude Code sessions; a human editing on GitHub web, or any other tool, bypasses them, and
235	those edits are caught at the **next session's** Stop sweep instead (the sweep judges the tree,
236	not the turn). A standing consumer workflow running the sweep was tried and retired by owner
237	decision (#385) — no GitHub Action rides the standard wiring; a repo that wants one can wire
238	`node .claudinite/shared/engine/checks/run.mjs` itself.
239	
240	**The conversation surface (Stop hook only).** Some process rules leave their artifact not in
241	the repo but in the *session itself* — e.g. `comment-classification` (the reply to the owner's
242	latest comment must declare an explicit `Comment class:` line: the assessment stays judgment,
243	but that an assessment was made is checkable) and `feature-requirements-first` (a
244	feature-classified comment's doc-first ordering on the branch). The Stop hook is the one surface
245	that can carry these: Claude Code passes the hook `transcript_path` on stdin, the hook forwards
246	it as `--transcript`, and `ctx.conversation()` exposes the parsed session
247	([helpers/session-transcript.mjs](helpers/session-transcript.mjs)). Everywhere else — CI, a manual run — the transcript
248	is absent and these rules self-gate to `[]`, the same self-supplied relevance gate skill checks
249	use. Two scoping choices keep them convergent rather than nagging: only the *latest* owner
250	comment is judged (a transcript is append-only, so an old omission could never be fixed), and
251	the ordering rule scopes to commits after the comment's timestamp (earlier work on the branch is
252	never re-litigated). The Stop hook's clean fast path also means a purely conversational turn —
253	no tracked change vs the base — runs no checks at all, conversation rules included.
254	
255	**Prefer a platform setting when one exists — but never trust it.** Squash-only merging is a
256	GitHub repo setting; force-push protection is branch protection. The setting does the
257	*enforcing*, but it's configuration like any other: it can be off, get switched off, or be
258	bypassed, and nothing notices. So every setting rung pairs with a check that verifies it, in
259	order of strength:

## Enforcement wiring

**A Stop hook is the guarantee; instructions are not.** Wiring the checks into `npm test` and
hoping the agent runs it is exactly the instruction-following this design escapes. Instead,
bootstrap registers one more hook in the consumer's `.claude/settings.json` (the same mechanism
as the existing SessionStart hooks):

1. The Stop hook fires when the agent finishes a turn.
2. It **fast-exits in milliseconds** when no tracked file differs from `main` — conversational
   turns cost nothing.
3. Otherwise it runs the full sweep; on findings it exits 2 with the findings on stderr.
   Claude Code blocks the stop and feeds that text back to the agent, which fixes the
   violations **in the same session**. A clean run stops silently.

Loop safety comes from convergence (fixed findings stop firing) plus Claude Code's own runaway
protection (it overrides a Stop hook after ~8 consecutive blocks).

**The Stop hook is the whole enforcement surface — consumers ship no CI job.** Hooks fire only
in Claude Code sessions; a human editing on GitHub web, or any other tool, bypasses them, and
those edits are caught at the **next session's** Stop sweep instead (the sweep judges the tree,
not the turn). A standing consumer workflow running the sweep was tried and retired by owner
decision (#385) — no GitHub Action rides the standard wiring; a repo that wants one can wire
`node .claudinite/shared/engine/checks/run.mjs` itself.

**The conversation surface (Stop hook only).** Some process rules leave their artifact not in
the repo but in the *session itself* — e.g. `comment-classification` (the reply to the owner's
latest comment must declare an explicit `Comment class:` line: the assessment stays judgment,
but that an assessment was made is checkable) and `feature-requirements-first` (a
feature-classified comment's doc-first ordering on the branch). The Stop hook is the one surface
that can carry these: Claude Code passes the hook `transcript_path` on stdin, the hook forwards
it as `--transcript`, and `ctx.conversation()` exposes the parsed session
([helpers/session-transcript.mjs](helpers/session-transcript.mjs)). Everywhere else — CI, a manual run — the transcript
is absent and these rules self-gate to `[]`, the same self-supplied relevance gate skill checks
use. Two scoping choices keep them convergent rather than nagging: only the *latest* owner
comment is judged (a transcript is append-only, so an old omission could never be fixed), and
the ordering rule scopes to commits after the comment's timestamp (earlier work on the branch is
never re-litigated). The Stop hook's clean fast path also means a purely conversational turn —
no tracked change vs the base — runs no checks at all, conversation rules included.

**Prefer a platform setting when one exists — but never trust it.** Squash-only merging is a
GitHub repo setting; force-push protection is branch protection. The setting does the
*enforcing*, but it's configuration like any other: it can be off, get switched off, or be
bypassed, and nothing notices. So every setting rung pairs with a check that verifies it, in
order of strength:

The two scopes fire at **different times**, because they answer different questions — one about
the change in front of the session, one about the repo as a whole:

- **Work scope → the Stop hook.** A repo's `.claude/settings.json` wires the stable
  [../hooks/stop-command.mjs](../hooks/stop-command.mjs) (see [bootstrap.md](../../bootstrap.md)),
  which fast-exits when nothing changed vs the base and otherwise runs
  [check_the_work.mjs](check_the_work.mjs) with the session transcript — the per-turn feedback
  loop, judging what the session just did (and the conversation-surface rules, which only exist
  at Stop). On blocking findings it exits 2 so the session fixes them before stopping.
  Self-limiting: after blocking twice on identical findings it lets the stop through.
- **World scope → the project's test/CI flow.** The whole-repo sweep is a repo-wide invariant
  assertion — the same kind of thing a test suite is — and is only *meaningful* at a
  commit/verify boundary, not every turn. So [check_the_world.mjs](check_the_world.mjs) is wired
  in as its own step wherever the project runs its tests (a CI job, a `make test` target, an npm
  script), invoked as the standalone `node …/check_the_world.mjs` command — **not** a
  language-specific test file, since a non-Node consumer's runner can't load one. Bootstrap wires
  this step during adoption, adding a minimal flow where the repo has none
  ([bootstrap.md](../../bootstrap.md)). This **supersedes** the earlier #385 stance ("no CI job;
  edits outside sessions surface at the next Stop sweep") — the world sweep now has a deterministic
  home in the test/CI flow rather than riding every Stop.

content.text: Now DESIGN.md — the "Enforcement wiring" section that this decision reverses:

input.old_string:
**A Stop hook is the guarantee; instructions are not.** Wiring the checks into `npm test` and
hoping the agent runs it is exactly the instruction-following this design escapes. Instead,
bootstrap registers one more hook in the consumer's `.claude/settings.json` (the same mechanism
as the existing SessionStart hooks):

1. The Stop hook fires when the agent finishes a turn.
2. It **fast-exits in milliseconds** when no tracked file differs from `main` — conversational
   turns cost nothing.
3. Otherwise it runs the full sweep; on findings it exits 2 with the findings on stderr.
   Claude Code blocks the stop and feeds that text back to the agent, which fixes the
   violations **in the same session**. A clean run stops silently.

Loop safety comes from convergence (fixed findings stop firing) plus Claude Code's own runaway
protection (it overrides a Stop hook after ~8 consecutive blocks).

**The Stop hook is the whole enforcement surface — consumers ship no CI job.** Hooks fire only
in Claude Code sessions; a human editing on GitHub web, or any other tool, bypasses them, and
those edits are caught at the **next session's** Stop sweep instead (the sweep judges the tree,
not the turn). A standing consumer workflow running the sweep was tried and retired by owner
decision (#385) — no GitHub Action rides the standard wiring; a repo that wants one can wire
`node .claudinite/shared/engine/checks/run.mjs` itself.
input.new_string:
The two scopes fire at **different times**, because they answer different questions. The *work*
scope judges the change in front of the session and belongs on a per-turn hook; the *world* scope
is a whole-repo invariant assertion — the same shape as a test suite — and belongs wherever the
project runs its tests, not on every turn.

**Work scope: a Stop hook is the guarantee; instructions are not.** Bootstrap registers one hook
in the consumer's `.claude/settings.json` (the same mechanism as the existing SessionStart hooks):

1. The Stop hook fires when the agent finishes a turn.
2. It **fast-exits in milliseconds** when no tracked file differs from `main` — conversational
   turns cost nothing.
3. Otherwise it runs the **work** sweep (`check_the_work.mjs`, with the session transcript); on
   findings it exits 2 with them on stderr. Claude Code blocks the stop and feeds that text back
   to the agent, which fixes the violations **in the same session**. A clean run stops silently.

Loop safety comes from convergence (fixed findings stop firing) plus Claude Code's own runaway
protection (it overrides a Stop hook after ~8 consecutive blocks).

**World scope: wired into the project's test/CI flow (revises #385).** The whole-repo sweep runs
as its own step wherever the project runs its tests — a CI job, a `make test` target, an npm
script — invoked as the standalone `node .claudinite/shared/engine/checks/check_the_world.mjs`
command. It is deliberately **not** a language-specific test file (a `*.test.mjs` a Node runner
discovers): a Python/Rust/Go consumer's suite could not load one, and the sweep is the engine's
own always-vendored Node CLI, so it runs as a step in any flow regardless of the project's
language. Bootstrap wires this step during adoption, adding a minimal flow where the repo has
none. This **supersedes the original #385 decision** ("the Stop hook is the whole enforcement
surface; consumers ship no CI job"): the earlier design kept the world sweep on Stop to spare
consumers a CI setup, but running a whole-repo sweep every turn is work misplaced in time, and a
human editing on GitHub web (or any non-session tool) is now caught by the test/CI flow the
change lands through rather than by the next session's Stop.

toolUseResult.originalFile:
# Adopting Claudinite

How a consuming repo adopts these shared guidelines, under the **vendored mount**
([vendoring/DESIGN.md](vendoring/DESIGN.md)): adoption is the **one network moment** — fetch the canon
once, vendor what this repo needs into **tracked files** under `.claudinite/shared/`, wire the
hooks — and every session after runs **offline** from the committed snapshot. The nightly
maintenance is the only regular updater. Idempotent: re-running refreshes the snapshot exactly
like a nightly would (fetch → converge → stamp) and never clobbers your own config — your own
`settings.json` entries are only ever added to, never overwritten.

## Part 1 — fetch the canon (the network moment)

```sh
scratch="$(mktemp -d)"
curl -fsSL https://codeload.github.com/missingbulb/Claudinite/tar.gz/main \
  | tar -xz --strip-components=1 -C "$scratch"
ref="$(git ls-remote https://github.com/missingbulb/Claudinite.git main 2>/dev/null | cut -f1)"
```

Adoption (and an on-demand refresh) is the only time a consumer fetches — sessions never do, so
the environment's network policy no longer needs `codeload.github.com` for day-to-day work.

## Part 2 — write the pack declaration (and run the adoption interview)

```sh
node "$scratch/engine/checks/check_the_world.mjs" --init
```

`--init` seeds `.claudinite-checks.json`: the baseline, the technology packs the repo's
fingerprint suspects, the default-on maintenance packs, each declared pack's `requires` closure,
and `"maintenance": { "delivery": "auto-merge" }`. A fingerprint only *suspects* a pack — from here on
the declaration is authoritative and adding/dropping packs is the project's call. Settings
**validity** is enforced at load: an unknown pack name, an unknown property, or malformed JSON is
a blocking `config` error.

If `--init` prints **pending adoption questions**, run the adoption interview now — this is the
one strict point (the owner is present by construction; outside bootstrap the same gap only ever
surfaces as a mild SessionStart note — [packs/README.md](packs/README.md#adoption-interview-questions)):
ask the owner each question via `AskUserQuestion`, record each answer **verbatim** on that pack's
entry as `answers: { "<question-id>": "<answer>" }` ("n/a — none wanted" is a valid answer), and
derive the entry's `config` where the question's distill note says how.

## Part 3 — vendor the snapshot

```sh
node "$scratch/vendoring/apply-vendor-set.mjs" --target . ${ref:+--ref "$ref"}
```

This materializes the repo's vendor set — the engine, the mount, the declared packs with their
skills, the corpus index — under `.claudinite/shared/` at canon-relative paths, and stamps the
declaration (`"claudinite": { "updated": "YYYY-MM-DD", "ref": "<sha>" }`). Whole-set convergence:
re-running it (or declaring a new pack and re-running) rebuilds the tree; errors abort before any
write. The `shared/` root is a **submodule emulation** — a future `git submodule add … .claudinite/shared`
lands a superset at the same path with no wiring change (see the design doc).

## Part 4 — track it

```sh
for rule in '/.claudinite-hooks.log' '/.claudinite-hooks.log.tmp'; do
  grep -qxF "$rule" .gitignore 2>/dev/null || echo "$rule" >> .gitignore
done
git add .gitignore .claudinite-checks.json .claudinite/shared
```

That is the **whole** ignore contract: the two hook-log lines. The vendored world writes
nothing untracked into `.claudinite/` — `shared/` and `local_packs/` are ordinary tracked
trees — so nothing there needs ignoring (#385).

## Part 5 — wire the hooks

Claude Code runs `SessionStart` entries **in parallel, in non-deterministic order**, so anything
sequenced must live inside **one** entry. Register exactly one — the orchestrator, which runs
the context steps in sequence in a single process (preferences → active-pack prose → skill
mounts → env check → interview check) and forwards their stdout into the session context:

```json
{ "hooks": { "SessionStart": [ { "hooks": [
  { "type": "command", "command": "bash $CLAUDE_PROJECT_DIR/.claudinite/shared/engine/hooks/session-start-command.sh" }
] } ] } }
```

Register the Stop hook (runs the conformance sweep when the session changed something; blocks
the stop while blocking findings remain) and the PreToolUse guard (deterministically blocks
forbidden commands) alongside it:

```json
{ "hooks": { "Stop": [ { "hooks": [
  { "type": "command", "command": "node $CLAUDE_PROJECT_DIR/.claudinite/shared/engine/hooks/stop-command.mjs" }
] } ],
  "PreToolUse": [ { "matcher": "Bash", "hooks": [
  { "type": "command", "command": "node $CLAUDE_PROJECT_DIR/.claudinite/shared/engine/hooks/pretooluse-command.mjs" }
] } ] } }
```

Invoke scripts **through `bash`/`node`**, never as bare paths — a dropped exec bit would fail the
hook before line 1 and swallow its own message. Notes on how the steps behave:

- **Preferences are fail-soft** — per-user content is never vendored;
  `shared/engine/hooks/inject-preferences.sh` reads a local copy where the tree has one and otherwise
  fetches the single `preferences/<email>.md`; any miss is a one-line note, and the session
  proceeds on defaults.
- **The halt-gate** — a SessionStart hook cannot block, but its stdout is injected into context,
  so a step that can't do its load-bearing job (`env.mjs check` — a missing toolchain) prints a
  plain-text directive telling the assistant to STOP and confirm via `AskUserQuestion` before any
  work. Plain text always — one hook's stdout must never mix JSON and prose.
- **The durable hook log** — every hook appends `start` / `done exit=N` lines to
  `.claudinite-hooks.log` at the repo root. No lines ⇒ the hook never triggered; `start` without
  `done` ⇒ it died executing. Reach for it first when a session says the harness didn't load.
- **Skill mounts are session-generated, never committed** — `shared/engine/skill_loader/mount-skills.mjs`
  (an orchestrator step) regenerates `.claude/skills/<name>` symlinks for the declared packs'
  union each session and maintains a self-ignoring `.gitignore` there; a committed link would
  dangle on every plain checkout.

One standing rule the vendored tree does **not** change: committed consumer code must not
`import`/`require` canon helpers from `.claudinite/` — the canon is refreshed nightly and
refactored upstream, so code reaching into it inherits every rename as a breaking change. Inline
what you need. The `claudinite-isolation` check enforces this outside the wiring files.

## Part 6 — request fleet enrollment (open one tracking issue)

A consuming project schedules nothing: the growth lifecycle and nightly maintenance run
centrally, from the owner's home repo — but only over repos on the routine's access list. So, as
part of a **first** adoption, open a GitHub issue in this repo's tracker assigned to
`missingbulb`, titled exactly **`Enroll <PROJECT_NAME> in Claudinite fleet maintenance`**
(idempotent: search first, skip if one exists, open or closed). Under the vendored mount this
matters more, not less: an unenrolled repo's snapshot simply freezes until someone refreshes it.
When the fleet's sweep baselines a repo it already maintains, it closes any still-open enrollment
issue — being reached proves enrollment.

## Part 7 — categorize the project (declare its class pack)

**Only for a fresh / empty project** — one without its own established working style. The owner
runs recurring **classes** of project, each carried by a project-class pack:

1. Ask the owner which class this project is, offering the project-class packs under
   [`packs/`](packs/) as the options.
2. A class pack fits → add its id to `"packs"` in `.claudinite-checks.json` and re-run Part 3
   (a declaration change triggers a whole-set refresh, so the new pack's content lands).
3. No class pack fits → run the project-instructions skill: it decomposes the project into pack
   facets and extracts its working instructions into new/refined canon packs (the primary
   deliverable) plus a thin project-specific overlay.

## Part 8 — land the adoption green

Run the sweep once and clear what it surfaces:

```sh
node .claudinite/shared/engine/checks/check_the_world.mjs
node .claudinite/shared/engine/checks/check_the_work.mjs
```

On a repo with existing code, **expect a backlog** — enforcement scope is whole-repo, and
findings in code you never touched would otherwise block every future session's Stop hook and
CI. Fix causes, or record a reasoned `accept` in `.claudinite-checks.json` for the deliberate
keeps. Don't reach for `--changed` to hide the backlog — it is a transitional aid, never the
enforcement default. Commit the adoption as one change (the vendored tree, the declaration, the
wiring) and push it through the normal PR flow.

## Part 9 — cloud environment setup (Claude Code on the web)

The web base image ships no toolchains; installs belong in the environment **image** (built
once, snapshotted), not a per-session hook. The corpus holds the one generic script —
[`engine/hooks/environment-setup-command.sh`](engine/hooks/environment-setup-command.sh), vendored into
`.claudinite/shared/engine/vendoring/` — identical for every project: paste its full body into the
environment's **Setup script** field and rebuild. It runs each active pack's declared installs
(`env.mjs install`, driven by the declaration); the SessionStart `env.mjs check` then only
*probes* and halt-gates on a genuinely missing prerequisite. The network policy must reach what
the active packs install from (npm registry, `pub.dev`, …) — the corpus itself is already in the
checkout.

## Bespoke merge policy (optional)

The portable merge recipe ships as the merge skill and needs nothing from you (squash via PR,
gate on CI only when the repo has it). Only if your project genuinely diverges: put the policy in
its own file and **name it explicitly in your `CLAUDE.md`** — the recipe reads it only then.

input.new_string:
## Part 8 — wire the world sweep into the test/CI flow, and land green

The **world-scope** sweep is a whole-repo invariant assertion — the same shape as a test suite —
so it runs wherever the project runs its tests, not on the Stop hook. Wire it in as its own step,
invoked as the standalone command (it is the engine's always-vendored Node CLI, so it runs in any
flow regardless of the project's own language — **never** add it as a language-specific test file
a runner discovers):

```sh
node .claudinite/shared/engine/checks/check_the_world.mjs
```

- **The project already has a test/CI flow** (a CI job, a `make test` target, an npm/pnpm
  `test` script, a `justfile`, …): add the command above as one more step, so a red world sweep
  fails that flow exactly like a failing test. Alongside it, run the **work** sweep too — in CI
  the git-based work rules (`reference-integrity`, `squash-merge-history`, `task-lifecycle`) still
  apply against the branch diff; the conversation rules self-skip with no transcript:
  ```sh
  node .claudinite/shared/engine/checks/check_the_work.mjs
  ```
- **The project has none:** add a **minimal** flow — a single CI job (or a `make`/script target)
  whose steps run the project's own tests, if any, then the two commands above. The point is a
  deterministic place the whole-repo sweep runs at each change; keep it as small as the repo needs.

Then run the world sweep once locally and clear what it surfaces. On a repo with existing code,
**expect a backlog** — enforcement scope is whole-repo, and findings in code you never touched
would otherwise fail every future run. Fix causes, or record a reasoned `accept` in
`.claudinite-checks.json` for the deliberate keeps. Don't reach for `--changed` to hide the
backlog — it is a transitional aid, never the enforcement default. Commit the adoption as one
change (the vendored tree, the declaration, the hook wiring, the test/CI step) and push it
through the normal PR flow.

---
name: adopt-claudinite
description: Bootstrap Claudinite into a consuming repo — mount, hooks, checks, skills. Use when asked to bootstrap, adopt, or set up Claudinite, or to baseline a repo to pick up updates.
---

Follow [bootstrap.md](../../../../bootstrap.md) — canonical there, and idempotent by design. Under the
vendored mount it is: fetch the canon once (the one network moment), `--init` the pack
declaration + run the adoption interview, vendor the snapshot into tracked
`.claudinite/shared/` (`vendoring/apply-vendor-set.mjs` — whole-set + stamp), track it, register the
single SessionStart orchestrator plus the Stop/PreToolUse hooks at their `shared/` paths, add
open the
maintenance-enrollment issue, categorize the project, and land the sweep green.

**Refreshing** an already-vendored repo on demand is the same flow minus the wiring: fetch a
fresh canon to scratch, run its `vendoring/apply-vendor-set.mjs` against the repo, commit the one
resulting change. **Never** hand-convert a legacy (fetch-at-session-start) member to the
vendored mount — that conversion is the gated flip note the nightly applies
([vendoring/DESIGN.md](../../../../vendoring/DESIGN.md), phase 2); until then legacy members are maintained
per bootstrap.md's transition appendix.

description: Bootstrap Claudinite into a consuming repo — mount, hooks, checks, skills. Use when asked to bootstrap, adopt, or set up Claudinite, or to baseline a repo to pick up updates.
---

Follow [bootstrap.md](../../../../bootstrap.md) — canonical there, and idempotent by design. Under the
vendored mount it is: fetch the canon once (the one network moment), `--init` the pack
declaration + run the adoption interview, vendor the snapshot into tracked

open the
maintenance-enrollment issue, categorize the project, and land the sweep green.

**Refreshing** an already-vendored repo on demand is the same flow minus the wiring: fetch a
fresh canon to scratch, run its `vendoring/apply-vendor-set.mjs` against the repo, commit the one
resulting change. **Never** hand-convert a legacy (fetch-at-session-start) member to the
vendored mount — that conversion is the gated flip note the nightly applies
([vendoring/DESIGN.md](../../../../vendoring/DESIGN.md), phase 2); until then legacy members are maintained
per bootstrap.md's transition appendix.
