# Claudinite

Portable Claude instructions/rules shared across projects — the **project-agnostic** maintainer guidelines, maintained **once** here and mounted into each consuming repo so they don't have to be copy-pasted per project. Project-*specific* rules stay in the consuming repo's own docs.

**Just adding Claudinite?** Tell the agent on the consuming repo:

> Read https://github.com/missingbulb/Claudinite/blob/main/README.md and bootstrap it.

---

## For the reading agent: how to traverse this corpus

The corpus map (maintainer-facing — there is deliberately no agent-facing corpus index, #385: a
session needs no architecture lesson, its rules arrive injected):

- **`packs/<name>/`** — a pack bundles everything it contributes: prose (`RULES.md`, injected at
  session start when the pack is declared), checks (run at every Stop), **and its skills**
  (`<pack>/skills/<skill>/` — activity-scoped procedures the harness surfaces on demand, one
  owning pack per skill). No pack is active undeclared — the baseline included; activation is
  the project's `.claudinite-checks.json` declaration ([packs/README.md](packs/README.md) —
  the one catalog; a pack's skills are listed nowhere but the pack, #385). A consumer's own
  packs sit in its `.claudinite/local_packs/<name>/`, same slots, same engine.
- **`engine/`** — the machinery that runs pack content, and the one always-vendored root:
  `engine/hooks/` (the wired SessionStart/PreToolUse entry points),
  `engine/checks/check_the_world.mjs` / `engine/checks/check_the_work.mjs` (the world- and
  work-scope conformance runners; the stable `engine/hooks/stop-command.mjs` Stop gate runs both), `engine/checks/helpers/`, `engine/pack_loader/`, `engine/pack_loader/`,
  `vendoring/` (vendoring). Design records: [engine/checks/DESIGN.md](engine/checks/DESIGN.md),
  [vendoring/DESIGN.md](vendoring/DESIGN.md); the core/pack boundary:
  [extending.md](extending.md).
- Before adding *any* rule as prose, run the promotion ladder in
  [engine/checks/DESIGN.md](engine/checks/DESIGN.md): a platform setting, a hook, a check, or a skill that can
  carry it beats prose.

---

## How consuming repos join

Consumers hold a **vendored, tracked** snapshot of the corpus at `.claudinite/shared/` — their
declaration-derived subset, committed as ordinary files, refreshed by the nightly maintenance as
one transactional commit (SessionStart hooks inject the active packs' prose; there is no corpus
index and no `@`-import — #385 — everything a session needs arrives injected, all offline). Adoption is
the one network moment. The model, its trade-offs, and the fleet transition from the earlier
fetch-at-session-start mount → [vendoring/DESIGN.md](vendoring/DESIGN.md); **setup steps →
[bootstrap.md](bootstrap.md)** (members on the legacy mount are converted by the gated flip
note, not by hand — bootstrap's transition appendix maintains them meanwhile).

## Repository operations

Beyond the portable corpus above, two folders hold the machinery that keeps it fed and tidy — Claudinite-internal orchestration, **not** part of the mounted corpus.

Before changing the canon itself, read **[consumer-safe-changes.md](consumer-safe-changes.md)** (provisional) — which propagation channel a change travels, how to migrate copied artifacts like stubs through the conformance checks, and the other practices we currently believe keep a canon change from hurting consuming repos. And before *adding* a feature, read **[extending.md](extending.md)** — the core/pack boundary (what's engine vs. pack-contributed content) and where each kind of new feature goes.

The **growth lifecycle** — how a lesson is learned in a consuming project, lifted into the canon when it's portable, and pruned back out once the canon owns it — is **fully pack-based**, three independent stages split across two packs by who declares them (no folder-access graph, no bespoke orchestrator step):

- The growth pack — the **member-side** stages, on every repo declaring the pack: **extract** (captures the last 24h of bugs/PRs/commits into the project's **own** docs via a PR that **auto-merges once its checks pass**), **dedup** (prunes local items the canon covers, keeping items the canon states too generally; opens a PR for review), and the weekly **pack discovery** pipeline.
- The curation pack — the **home-only** pack, declared solely by the canon home repo, so its tasks run central-once by declaration cardinality: **promote** (reads the changed members' local docs, **generalizes** the portable lessons, routes each to the right canon home, and opens a PR against Claudinite's `main` — the sole judgment gate before shared canon; it replaced the old cross-repo handoff, Action + PAT + labelled issue, which is gone) with its shared **item-routing** method, and the weekly **prose-mining sweep** task.

The mounted corpus itself is **`packs/`** — each `packs/<name>/` bundling a pack's prose `RULES.md`, its check modules, and its skills, discovered structurally by [engine/pack_loader/pack-registry.mjs](engine/pack_loader/pack-registry.mjs) and activated by declaration. `engine/` holds only the machinery that runs pack content. Usage and configuration → [engine/checks/README.md](engine/checks/README.md); design → [engine/checks/DESIGN.md](engine/checks/DESIGN.md); the per-rule audit → [docs/conversion-inventory.md](docs/conversion-inventory.md).

`migrations/` holds the **baseline migrations** mechanism — declared, self-retiring path relocations, one record per in-flight canon rename (a renamed or relocated artifact consumers hold their own copy of) that supplies the read-side resolver, the write-side rename, and the fleet telemetry that auto-retires it once every consumer has moved. Named for when it runs: baselining applies and retires each. See [migrations/README.md](migrations/README.md).

`routines/` holds the scheduled jobs:

- [routines/auto-all-repos-maintenance.md](routines/auto-all-repos-maintenance.md) — **the single scheduled entry point**, run daily from the fleet-**enforcer** repo (one that declares the enforcer pack). It runs the core planner over the repos it can reach (the home repo included, planned last), reads the emitted units, and runs each covered repo's due `run_daily` units at their `smarts` tier — no repo or unit can stop the others. Schedule **this**, nothing else.
- [routines/fleet/plan.mjs](routines/fleet/plan.mjs) — the **planner**: pack-agnostic core code that goes over the repos it can reach and, for each covered member, assembles its declared packs' `run_daily` tasks, runs each gate in code, and emits the day's work plan. It depends on no single pack — an enforcer pack's coverage census is a separate, isolated concern, never the source of the plan.
- `check-fleet-coverage.mjs` — the enforcer pack's **census** (the cross-repo reach the pack adds): with an account-spanning PAT it enumerates the owner's repos, classifies coverage, and converges one adoption issue per uncovered repo. It is **coverage, not planning** — it does not build the work plan, and it carries **no migration logic** (baseline-migration application and retirement are the migrations flow's own standalone passes, `migrations/fleet-apply.mjs` + `migrations/fleet-retire.mjs`). Dispatched via that repo's `workflow_dispatch`-only coverage workflow (materialized from the pack's stub); which repos to cover and the exclude list come from its pack entry's `config`.
- [routines/fleet/](routines/fleet/DESIGN.md) — the **run_daily engine** (the code that decides each repo's due tasks from its active packs) and the scheduling contract. **Baselining** is now the baseline pack's daily task; **growth** rides the member-side pack and the home-side pack; the PR/branch/issue sweep is the repo-tidy pack.

**Where things land:** **extract** lands each project's lessons through an **auto-merging PR** — CI-gated but not human-reviewed, so daily capture never queues up (it writes only local docs) — while **promote** (the canon gate) and **dedup** each open a PR for the owner to approve. Every stage reads only what's already merged: a lesson extracted tonight is promoted the next night (the extract PR's merge shows up in the next night's local-pack signal), and promote's PR means promote → dedup waits an approval cycle — the dominant latency either way. An owner-requested, in-session retrospective delivers a PR for a human to review, not one that self-merges.

## The submodule future (for consumers)

The vendored `shared/` root deliberately mirrors this repo's layout so that mounting Claudinite
as a **git submodule at `.claudinite/shared/`** — once sessions run where a cross-repo git
credential exists — is a drop-in upgrade that changes no wiring. Details in
[vendoring/DESIGN.md](vendoring/DESIGN.md).

+  work-scope conformance runners — world runs in the project's test/CI flow, work in the stable

toolUseResult.originalFile:
# The CANON repo's own CI — Claudinite's test suite + its own conformance sweep
# on every PR. Not consumer wiring (consumers ship no workflow, #385).
name: CI
on:
  pull_request:
  push:
    branches: [main]
defaults:
  run:
    shell: bash
jobs:
  test-and-conformance:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
        with:
          fetch-depth: 0
          # Check out the PR's real head, not the synthetic refs/pull/N/merge commit
          # actions/checkout uses by default — otherwise HEAD is an ephemeral merge of
          # the branch into main, which the work-scoped squash-merge-history check would
          # (correctly, for that synthetic commit) flag. This makes CI evaluate exactly
          # what the local Stop hook does: the branch tip. Falls back to github.sha on push.
          ref: ${{ github.event.pull_request.head.sha || github.sha }}
      - uses: actions/setup-node@v4
        with:
          node-version: 20
      - name: Unit tests
        # engine-tests/ mirrors engine/'s inner structure (#385 — engine/ ships to consumers exactly as-is, tests stay canon-side); each pack carries its own tests inside its tree, the home's local packs included; routines/fleet/ carries the planner tests.
        # nullglob: a location with no tests drops out instead of reaching node as a literal pattern.
        run: |
          shopt -s nullglob
          tests=(engine-tests/*.test.mjs engine-tests/*/*.test.mjs packs/*.test.mjs packs/*/*.test.mjs packs/*/skills/*/*.test.mjs routines/*/*.test.mjs .claudinite/local_packs/*/*.test.mjs .claudinite/local_packs/*/skills/*/*.test.mjs)
          node --test "${tests[@]}"
      - name: Conformance sweep
        # Both scope runners: world (repo-state rules) and work (rules judging
        # this branch's change) — the same pair the Stop hook runs.
        run: |
          node engine/checks/check_the_world.mjs
          node engine/checks/check_the_work.mjs
      - name: Promote write-surface gate
        # The growth promote stage (.claudinite/local_packs/canon-curation/promote.md) is the one change
        # bounded to packs/ + skills/ — an ordinary engine PR legitimately edits checks/,
        # routines/, so this cannot run on every PR. It keys on the promote branch prefix
        # (promote.md pins it) and fails the promote PR if a changed path strays.
        if: contains(github.head_ref, 'growth-promote')
        run: node .claudinite/local_packs/canon-curation/promote-scope.mjs

 ## Enforcement wiring
 
-**A Stop hook is the guarantee; instructions are not.** Wiring the checks into `npm test` and
-hoping the agent runs it is exactly the instruction-following this design escapes. Instead,
-bootstrap registers one more hook in the consumer's `.claude/settings.json` (the same mechanism
-as the existing SessionStart hooks):
+The two scopes fire at **different times**, because they answer different questions. The *work*
+scope judges the change in front of the session and belongs on a per-turn hook; the *world* scope
+is a whole-repo invariant assertion — the same shape as a test suite — and belongs wherever the
+project runs its tests, not on every turn.
+
+**Work scope: a Stop hook is the guarantee; instructions are not.** Bootstrap registers one hook
+in the consumer's `.claude/settings.json` (the same mechanism as the existing SessionStart hooks):
 
 1. The Stop hook fires when the agent finishes a turn.
 2. It **fast-exits in milliseconds** when no tracked file differs from `main` — conversational
    turns cost nothing.
-3. Otherwise it runs the full sweep; on findings it exits 2 with the findings on stderr.
-   Claude Code blocks the stop and feeds that text back to the agent, which fixes the
-   violations **in the same session**. A clean run stops silently.
+3. Otherwise it runs the **work** sweep (`check_the_work.mjs`, with the session transcript); on
+   findings it exits 2 with them on stderr. Claude Code blocks the stop and feeds that text back
+   to the agent, which fixes the violations **in the same session**. A clean run stops silently.
 
 Loop safety comes from convergence (fixed findings stop firing) plus Claude Code's own runaway
 protection (it overrides a Stop hook after ~8 consecutive blocks).
 
-**The Stop hook is the whole enforcement surface — consumers ship no CI job.** Hooks fire only
-in Claude Code sessions; a human editing on GitHub web, or any other tool, bypasses them, and
-those edits are caught at the **next session's** Stop sweep instead (the sweep judges the tree,
-not the turn). A standing consumer workflow running the sweep was tried and retired by owner
-decision (#385) — no GitHub Action rides the standard wiring; a repo that wants one can wire
-`node .claudinite/shared/engine/checks/run.mjs` itself.
+**World scope: wired into the project's test/CI flow (revises #385).** The whole-repo sweep runs
+as its own step wherever the project runs its tests — a CI job, a `make test` target, an npm
+script — invoked as the standalone `node .claudinite/shared/engine/checks/check_the_world.mjs`
+command. It is deliberately **not** a language-specific test file (a `*.test.mjs` a Node runner
+discovers): a Python/Rust/Go consumer's suite could not load one, and the sweep is the engine's
+own always-vendored Node CLI, so it runs as a step in any flow regardless of the project's
+language. Bootstrap wires this step during adoption, adding a minimal flow where the repo has
+none. This **supersedes the original #385 decision** ("the Stop hook is the whole enforcement
+surface; consumers ship no CI job"): the earlier design kept the world sweep on Stop to spare
+consumers a CI setup, but running a whole-repo sweep every turn is work misplaced in time, and a
+human editing on GitHub web (or any non-session tool) is now caught by the test/CI flow the
+change lands through rather than by the next session's Stop.
 
 **The conversation surface (Stop hook only).** Some process rules leave their artifact not in
 the repo but in the *session itself* — e.g. `comment-classification` (the reply to the owner's

input.body:
Closes #392.

## What

Splits Claudinite's conformance checks into two scopes that fire at **different times**, gives the check-the-work rules a fluent context, and moves the whole-repo sweep off the Stop hook into the project's test/CI flow.

### Fluent `work` context + scope split (commits 1–3)
- `engine/checks/helpers/work.mjs` — a null-safe fluent view over `ctx`. A rule declares `scope: 'work'` and its signature becomes `run(work)`; `runRule(rule, ctx)` is the single dispatch seam (runner + every rule test). API: `work.conversation().ownerTurns().last()` (null-object turn — no guard ladders), `work.jsonPair(file)`, `work.deadLinks()`, `work.danglingReferences(tolerated)`, `work.addedLines()`, `work.branchCommits()`, `work.introducedMerges()`, `work.filesContaining()`, plus delegated raw surface (`files`, `read`, `exists`, `branch`, `commits`, `packConfig(id)`).
- `ctx.read` / `ctx.readBase` cached per sweep (read-only over an immutable snapshot; each `readBase` was a `git show`).
- Runner split into `check_the_world.mjs` (world rules + settings/interview diagnostics) and `check_the_work.mjs` (rules judging the change), over a shared `sweep.mjs`.
- Eight work rules converted (`comment-classification`, `squash-merge-history`, `reference-integrity`, `task-lifecycle`, `feature-requirements-first`, `node/earn-each-dependency`, `cer/permission-added-store-issue`, `google-client-id-single-origin`), with full `work`/`lastOwnerTurn`/`lastFeatureTurn` naming (no abbreviations) and preamble comments trimmed.

### World → test/CI flow; Stop → work only (commit 4)
The work sweep judges the change in front of the session → stays on the **Stop hook** (per-turn, with the transcript). The world sweep is a whole-repo invariant assertion — the shape of a test suite — so it runs wherever the project runs its tests, as its own standalone `node …/check_the_world.mjs` **step** (never a language-specific test file: a non-Node consumer's runner can't load one, and the sweep is the engine's always-vendored Node CLI).
- `stop-command.mjs` now runs only `check_the_work.mjs` — no more spawning both runners every turn.
- Bootstrap wires the world step into the project's test/CI flow, adding a minimal flow where the repo has none.
- This **supersedes the original #385 decision** (Stop as the whole enforcement surface, no consumer CI). DESIGN.md, `engine/checks/README.md`, `bootstrap.md`, the `adopt-claudinite` skill, and top-level README are updated; canon `ci.yml` models the pattern.

## Verification
- Full suite green throughout: **491 tests** (added `engine-tests/hooks/stop-command.test.mjs`, pinning that Stop surfaces work findings and *not* world ones).
- See-it-fail by mutation at each step: `Turns.last`, `danglingReferences`, `jsonPair` base side, the `runRule` scope dispatch, and the Stop-hook work-only behavior each made existing fixtures bite; every mutation restored byte-identical.
- End-to-end: on a fixture carrying both a world (`warning-suppression`) and a work (`reference-integrity`) violation — `check_the_world` reports only the world one, `check_the_work` only the work ones, and the Stop hook surfaces only work (exit 2). Both sweeps run clean on this repo.

Also removed three broken `.claude/skills/` symlinks (pre-#385 layout leftovers).

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)

https://claude.ai/code/session_015EGXi3y8hZNfS7A9csREv1

content.text: {"id":"4107354760","url":"https://github.com/missingbulb/Claudinite/pull/393"}

Comment class: process-change — I implemented the enforcement-timing change you directed (world → test/CI flow, Stop → work only) across code, the bootstrap contract, and the design record.

What landed (commit `0f12689`, pushed to [PR #393](https://github.com/missingbulb/Claudinite/pull/393)):

- **`stop-command.mjs` runs `check_the_work` only** — the per-turn double-spawn is gone. Verified end-to-end: a fixture with both a `warning-suppression` (world) and a `reference-integrity` (work) violation surfaces only the work one at Stop.
- **World is wired into the test/CI flow** as a standalone `node …/check_the_world.mjs` step — explicitly *not* a `*.test.mjs`, exactly per your non-Node point: it's the engine's always-vendored Node CLI, so it runs as a step in any flow (a CI job, `make test`, an npm script) regardless of the project's language.
- **Bootstrap does the wiring** — `bootstrap.md` Part 8 now adds the world step to the project's existing test/CI flow, and **adds a minimal flow where the repo has none** (your chosen option). The `adopt-claudinite` skill's checklist names it too.
- **The #385 reversal is recorded, not silent** — DESIGN.md's "Stop hook is the whole enforcement surface" section, `engine/checks/README.md`, and the top-level README now say world lives in the test/CI flow and *why* it supersedes #385. The canon `ci.yml` models the pattern.
- **Regression test added** — `engine-tests/hooks/stop-command.test.mjs` pins that Stop surfaces work findings and not world ones; see-it-fail confirmed by making Stop run world (2 failures), then restored byte-identical.

Full suite: **491 green**. Both sweeps clean on this repo.

> ✅ All done — world sweep now runs in the test/CI flow, Stop carries the work scope only, bootstrap wires it (minimal flow where none exists), the #385 reversal is documented, and it's pushed to PR #393. Say "LGTM" to merge, or point me at the next piece.

attachment.content:
# Owner interaction preferences

Personal preferences from the repo owner for how the assistant should interact — tone, summary style, end-of-turn conventions, how results and decisions are surfaced, and the phrases the owner uses to trigger defined commands. These are **not** project conventions (those live in the [packs/](../packs/) corpus, each pack bundling its skills); they're how the owner likes to be worked with. Where a preference triggers a command whose mechanics are a project convention, the mechanics stay in their own doc and the entry here just owns the trigger phrase.

## Preferences

- **When there's nothing left to do, say so boldly at the end of the conversation.** Don't bury a "this is complete / I'm blocked / nothing further to do" behind hedging — make the terminal state unmistakable. **Set it apart visually so it catches the eye:** put the terminal state — or, when work remains, the list of open/outstanding items — in its own callout (a Markdown blockquote box, e.g. `> ✅ All done` or `> ⚠️ Still open: …`), not trailing prose the owner has to scan for. One or the other always gets the box: the done-signal when nothing remains, the open-items list when something does.
- **"LGTM"** is the owner's phrase for the merge-to-main command: it asks for the change in front of the owner to be merged into `main`. It's an owner-triggered command; the default mechanics live in the `merge-to-main` skill ([../packs/git-github/skills/merge-to-main/SKILL.md](../packs/git-github/skills/merge-to-main/SKILL.md)) (squash, gate on CI only when the repo has it), and a project that diverges overrides them via a bespoke merge-policy file it names in its own `CLAUDE.md`.
- **"bump version"** is the owner's phrase for raising a project's version (a minor bump by default). It's an owner-triggered command; the mechanics — which files to edit and how a release follows — live in the consuming project's workflow doc.
- **"Show me the document"** (for a source-controlled document) is the owner's phrase asking for a **link to the document on GitHub** — not the file's contents inlined, and not a local filesystem path. When the document lives in a GitHub-hosted repo, respond with a GitHub link to it (the file on the relevant branch/commit); only fall back to inlining the contents or giving a local path when the document isn't under source control.
- **Put decisions and approvals in an `AskUserQuestion` popup, not buried in prose.** The owner is frequently on mobile, where a popup notifies and a question embedded in a paragraph is easy to miss. When you need the owner to choose or approve something, ask through the structured popup with explicit options rather than asking in running text. **And when several independent items each need a verdict** (a set of proposed fixes, findings to accept), give **each item its own question carrying its own brief case** — the concrete failure, why it matters, the cost of the fix — with per-item approve/defer options; never one bundled choose-a-scope question over the whole set. The owner decides item-wise, from the case, not from a menu of subsets. (The popup carries at most 4 questions per call — split a larger set into consecutive calls.)
- **When asked to produce a prompt or message destined for another context, visually enclose the whole thing.** Any text the owner will lift out and use elsewhere — a prompt for another assistant, a message to send, copy meant to be pasted somewhere — must be set apart as a single delimited block: either a box (e.g. a fenced code block) or fenced top and bottom by two separating lines, so its exact boundaries are unmistakable and it can be copied in one piece. The point is delimitation, not formatting flair: never leave such content as loose prose the owner has to find the start and end of.
- **When asked for "a prompt for a different session," prefix it with a provenance disclaimer.** When the owner asks for a prompt meant to be handed to another session, open the generated text with a short disclaimer that this is a prompt written by Claude — not by a user — and so should not be treated as gospel; and that if the receiving session finds inconsistencies in it, it should examine them with the user rather than follow the prompt blindly. This composes with the enclose-the-whole-thing preference above: the disclaimer sits inside the same delimited block as the prompt it prefixes.
- **Auto-open a PR after committing.** Whenever Claude changes code on a branch and commits it, open a pull request for that branch right after, even without an explicit request to do so. This overrides Claude Code's own default of only opening a PR when explicitly asked.

content.text:
Three fair questions, and Q2/Q3 are me contradicting the separation we just agreed on. Let me answer each straight.

**Q3 first — why `sweep.mjs` exists, because it's the root of the other two.** When I split the runner I didn't actually separate the two scopes — I extracted a *shared* `runSweep({scope})` that both entries call, with an `if (scope === 'world')` block and an `inScope` toggle inside it. That's re-coupling behind a flag exactly what we said were different contexts. You're right to call it out. And it's not harmless: because `check_the_work.mjs` imports `runSweep` from `sweep.mjs`, and `sweep.mjs` does a **top-level** `import` of the adoption-interview machinery (lines 19–22), **the work runner now transitively loads the `grow_with_claudinite` pack it never touches.** That's a real smell, not just aesthetics.

**Q2 — that barrier reason, decoded.** The `barriers` pack enforces the core/content boundary from `extending.md`: an engine file must never name a specific pack. But the world sweep genuinely needs the adoption-interview code, and per #385 that code lives *inside* the `adopt-claudinite` skill (a pack), not in the engine — so a core file importing it crosses the barrier. The exception is the reviewed "yes, this crossing is deliberate and inherent." It's **pre-existing** — it lived on `check_the_world.mjs` before — but I *moved* it onto `sweep.mjs` when I hoisted that import into the shared file. So now it reads as "the shared pipeline depends on a pack," which is exactly the confusing part. The reason string is also just badly written. Fixing Q3 relocates this back to the world runner, where "the world sweep owns adoption diagnostics" is legible.

**Q1 — why both runners in CI.** CI is the merge gate — the one surface a change from *outside* a Claude session (a human PR, an external tool) passes through. The Stop hook only fires in sessions, so if CI ran world only, the git-based work rules (`reference-integrity`, `squash-merge-history`, `task-lifecycle`) would never check such a PR — a human PR could delete a file and leave dangling references uncaught. So work-in-CI is a deliberate backstop, not a leftover. It's consistent with "different timings" (the rule we set is *world is never per-turn*; work legitimately wants both fast-feedback at Stop and the authoritative merge-gate at CI) — but I owe you the choice, since it does mean work runs on two surfaces.

The root fix for Q2+Q3 is the corpus's own rule — *shared helpers carry mechanism, not policy*: extract only the scope-blind mechanism, and let the world runner own its world-only diagnostics (and the pack import, and the exception). Two genuine forks for you:

questions.question: How should the shared runner code be restructured so work and world aren't re-coupled behind a scope flag?

options.label: Scope-blind mechanism helper (Recommended)
options.description: Delete runSweep(scope). Keep only a scope-blind helper — 'run the active packs' rules matching a predicate, render + count' — with NO scope branch and NO pack imports. check_the_work.mjs passes r=>r.scope==='work'; check_the_world.mjs passes r=>r.scope!=='work' AND owns the settings/interview diagnostics, the grow_with_claudinite import, and the barrier exception. The work runner stops depending on that pack entirely. Matches 'helpers carry mechanism, not policy.'

options.label: Full duplication, no shared file
options.description: Delete sweep.mjs outright. Each runner is self-contained top-to-bottom (~15 lines of pack-iteration loop duplicated between them). Maximal separation and each file reads as its own thing, but the duplicated loop can drift and a future rule-dispatch change must be made in two places.

questions.question: Should CI keep running check_the_work (the merge-gate backstop for non-session PRs), or run world only?

options.label: Keep work in CI (Recommended)
options.description: CI runs both. The git-based work rules (reference-integrity, squash-merge-history, task-lifecycle) catch a human/external PR that never hit a Stop hook; conversation rules self-skip with no transcript. Cost: work runs on two surfaces (Stop + CI).

options.label: World only in CI
options.description: Pure separation: work=Stop, world=CI, each on exactly one surface. Cost: a non-session PR's work-scope violations (e.g. a dangling reference from a human file deletion, a stray merge commit) go uncaught until a later Claude session runs its Stop hook.

        # The world sweep's home is the test/CI flow (not the Stop hook, which

toolUseResult.originalFile:
# The CANON repo's own CI — Claudinite's test suite + its own conformance sweep
# on every PR. Not consumer wiring (consumers ship no workflow, #385).
name: CI
on:
  pull_request:
  push:
    branches: [main]
defaults:
  run:
    shell: bash
jobs:
  test-and-conformance:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
        with:
          fetch-depth: 0
          # Check out the PR's real head, not the synthetic refs/pull/N/merge commit
          # actions/checkout uses by default — otherwise HEAD is an ephemeral merge of
          # the branch into main, which the work-scoped squash-merge-history check would
          # (correctly, for that synthetic commit) flag. This makes CI evaluate exactly
          # what the local Stop hook does: the branch tip. Falls back to github.sha on push.
          ref: ${{ github.event.pull_request.head.sha || github.sha }}
      - uses: actions/setup-node@v4
        with:
          node-version: 20
      - name: Unit tests
        # engine-tests/ mirrors engine/'s inner structure (#385 — engine/ ships to consumers exactly as-is, tests stay canon-side); each pack carries its own tests inside its tree, the home's local packs included; routines/fleet/ carries the planner tests.
        # nullglob: a location with no tests drops out instead of reaching node as a literal pattern.
        run: |
          shopt -s nullglob
          tests=(engine-tests/*.test.mjs engine-tests/*/*.test.mjs packs/*.test.mjs packs/*/*.test.mjs packs/*/skills/*/*.test.mjs routines/*/*.test.mjs .claudinite/local_packs/*/*.test.mjs .claudinite/local_packs/*/skills/*/*.test.mjs)
          node --test "${tests[@]}"
      - name: Conformance sweep
        # The world sweep's home is the test/CI flow (not the Stop hook, which
        # carries only the work scope) — this canon repo models the pattern
        # bootstrap wires into consumers. Work runs here too: in CI the git-based
        # work rules apply against the branch diff; the conversation rules self-skip.
        run: |
          node engine/checks/check_the_world.mjs
          node engine/checks/check_the_work.mjs
      - name: Promote write-surface gate
        # The growth promote stage (.claudinite/local_packs/canon-curation/promote.md) is the one change
        # bounded to packs/ + skills/ — an ordinary engine PR legitimately edits checks/,
        # routines/, so this cannot run on every PR. It keys on the promote branch prefix
        # (promote.md pins it) and fails the promote PR if a changed path strays.
        if: contains(github.head_ref, 'growth-promote')
        run: node .claudinite/local_packs/canon-curation/promote-scope.mjs

input.old_string:
- **The project already has a test/CI flow** (a CI job, a `make test` target, an npm/pnpm
  `test` script, a `justfile`, …): add the command above as one more step, so a red world sweep
  fails that flow exactly like a failing test. Alongside it, run the **work** sweep too — in CI
  the git-based work rules (`reference-integrity`, `squash-merge-history`, `task-lifecycle`) still
  apply against the branch diff; the conversation rules self-skip with no transcript:
  ```sh
  node .claudinite/shared/engine/checks/check_the_work.mjs
  ```
- **The project has none:** add a **minimal** flow — a single CI job (or a `make`/script target)
  whose steps run the project's own tests, if any, then the two commands above. The point is a
  deterministic place the whole-repo sweep runs at each change; keep it as small as the repo needs.

-  vs the base; on blocking findings exits 2 so the session fixes them before stopping.
+The two scopes fire at **different times**, because they answer different questions — one about
+the change in front of the session, one about the repo as a whole:
+
+- **Work scope → the Stop hook.** A repo's `.claude/settings.json` wires the stable
+  [../hooks/stop-command.mjs](../hooks/stop-command.mjs) (see [bootstrap.md](../../bootstrap.md)),
+  which fast-exits when nothing changed vs the base and otherwise runs
+  [check_the_work.mjs](check_the_work.mjs) with the session transcript — the per-turn feedback
+  loop, judging what the session just did (and the conversation-surface rules, which only exist
+  at Stop). On blocking findings it exits 2 so the session fixes them before stopping.
   Self-limiting: after blocking twice on identical findings it lets the stop through.
-- **No CI job in the standard wiring** — edits made outside Claude sessions surface at the next
-  session's Stop sweep (owner decision, #385). A repo that wants a CI backstop can run
-  `node engine/checks/check_the_world.mjs` itself; same sweep, same messages.
+- **World scope → the project's test/CI flow.** The whole-repo sweep is a repo-wide invariant
+  assertion — the same kind of thing a test suite is — and is only *meaningful* at a
+  commit/verify boundary, not every turn. So [check_the_world.mjs](check_the_world.mjs) is wired
+  in as its own step wherever the project runs its tests (a CI job, a `make test` target, an npm
+  script), invoked as the standalone `node …/check_the_world.mjs` command — **not** a
+  language-specific test file, since a non-Node consumer's runner can't load one. Bootstrap wires
+  this step during adoption, adding a minimal flow where the repo has none
+  ([bootstrap.md](../../bootstrap.md)). This **supersedes** the earlier #385 stance ("no CI job;
+  edits outside sessions surface at the next Stop sweep") — the world sweep now has a deterministic
+  home in the test/CI flow rather than riding every Stop.
 
 ## Adding a rule

input.body:
Closes #392.

## What

Reworks Claudinite's conformance checks into **two fully independent scope runners** that fire at **different times**, and gives the check-the-work rules a fluent context.

### Fluent `work` context
- `engine/checks/helpers/work.mjs` — a null-safe fluent view over `ctx`. A rule declares `scope: 'work'` and its signature becomes `run(work)`; `runRule(rule, ctx)` is the single dispatch seam. API: `work.conversation().ownerTurns().last()` (null-object turn — no guard ladders), `work.jsonPair(file)`, `work.deadLinks()`, `work.danglingReferences(tolerated)`, `work.addedLines()`, `work.branchCommits()`, `work.introducedMerges()`, `work.filesContaining()`, plus delegated raw surface. Full `work`/`lastOwnerTurn`/`lastFeatureTurn` naming, no abbreviations.
- `ctx.read` / `ctx.readBase` cached per sweep.
- Eight work rules converted: `comment-classification`, `squash-merge-history`, `reference-integrity`, `task-lifecycle`, `feature-requirements-first`, `node/earn-each-dependency`, `cer/permission-added-store-issue`, `google-client-id-single-origin`.

### Two independent runners
`check_the_world.mjs` and `check_the_work.mjs` are fully independent — **neither imports the other or any shared runner**. They share only two scope-blind mechanism helpers with descriptive names:
- `run-active-pack-rules.mjs` — run the active packs' rules a predicate admits (each runner passes its own scope predicate; no scope logic in the helper).
- `report-findings.mjs` — apply config, sort, print, return the blocking count.

The world runner alone carries the settings/interview diagnostics, the `adopt-claudinite` interview import, and the `barriers` core/content exception (which now names `check_the_world.mjs` with a legible reason). The work runner has **zero pack dependency** — the barrier check actively guards this (a pack import into it is flagged BLOCKING; verified by mutation).

> An earlier revision extracted a shared `sweep.mjs` with a `runSweep(scope)` that branched on scope internally — that re-coupled the two scopes and made the work runner transitively depend on the `grow_with_claudinite` pack via a top-level interview import. That file is deleted; this is the fix.

### Different timings
- **Work → the Stop hook.** `stop-command.mjs` runs `check_the_work.mjs` only — the per-turn feedback loop, with the transcript, judging the session's change (and the conversation-surface rules, which only exist at Stop).
- **World → the project's test/CI flow.** The whole-repo sweep runs as its own standalone `node …/check_the_world.mjs` step wherever the project runs its tests — **not** a language-specific test file (a non-Node consumer's runner can't load one; it's the engine's always-vendored Node CLI). Bootstrap wires it in, adding a minimal flow where the repo has none. **CI runs the world sweep only** — the work scope belongs to the session's Stop, not CI.
- This **supersedes the original #385 decision** (Stop as the whole enforcement surface, no consumer CI). DESIGN.md, `engine/checks/README.md`, `bootstrap.md`, the `adopt-claudinite` skill, and the top-level README are updated; canon `ci.yml` models the pattern (world only).

## Verification
- Full suite green throughout: **491 tests** (added `engine-tests/hooks/stop-command.test.mjs`, pinning that Stop surfaces work findings and *not* world ones).
- See-it-fail by mutation at each step: `Turns.last`, `danglingReferences`, `jsonPair` base side, the `runRule` scope dispatch, the Stop-hook work-only behavior, and the barrier guard on the work runner's independence each made a check bite; every mutation restored byte-identical.
- End-to-end: on a fixture carrying both a world (`warning-suppression`) and a work (`reference-integrity`) violation — `check_the_world` reports only the world one, `check_the_work` only the work ones, and the Stop hook surfaces only work. `--list`/`--init` intact. Both sweeps run clean on this repo (0 blocking).

Also removed three broken `.claude/skills/` symlinks (pre-#385 layout leftovers).

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)

https://claude.ai/code/session_015EGXi3y8hZNfS7A9csREv1

content.text: {"id":"4107354760","url":"https://github.com/missingbulb/Claudinite/pull/393"}

Comment class: correction (Q2/Q3 — I'd built a shared scope-branching pipeline that contradicted the separation we agreed on) plus one decision (Q1).

Answers and what I did:

**Q3 — `sweep.mjs`.** You were right: it was a single `runSweep(scope)` that branched on scope internally — re-coupling exactly what the split was supposed to separate. **Deleted.** The two runners are now fully independent — neither imports the other. They share only two scope-blind helpers, named as you asked (verb+noun, descriptive):
- `run-active-pack-rules.mjs` — runs the active packs' rules a caller's predicate admits (each runner passes its own scope predicate; the helper contains no scope logic).
- `report-findings.mjs` — applies config, sorts, prints, returns the blocking count.

**Q2 — that barrier reason.** It was the symptom. Because `sweep.mjs` did a top-level `import` of the adoption-interview machinery (which lives inside the `adopt-claudinite` skill per #385), and *both* runners imported `sweep.mjs`, the **work runner transitively depended on the `grow_with_claudinite` pack it never uses** — and the core/content barrier exception landed on the shared file, reading as "the pipeline depends on a pack." Now the interview import lives in `check_the_world.mjs` alone (it's the runner that emits adoption diagnostics), the exception moved back there with a rewritten legible reason, and the work runner has **zero pack dependency**. The barrier check actively guards that — I verified a pack import into `check_the_work.mjs` is flagged BLOCKING.

**Q1 — both runners in CI.** Your call: **world only in CI now.** `ci.yml`, `bootstrap.md`, and the README no longer run or document work in CI. The tradeoff I flagged stands (a non-session PR's work-scope violations aren't caught until a later session's Stop) — that's the accepted cost of the clean separation.

491 tests green; both sweeps clean (0 blocking); `--list`/`--init` intact. Pushed to [PR #393](https://github.com/missingbulb/Claudinite/pull/393).

> ✅ All done — `sweep.mjs` gone, the two runners are independent (barrier-enforced), CI runs world only, and the interview import + exception sit where they're legible. Say "LGTM" to merge, or point me at the next thing.

attachment.content:
# Owner interaction preferences

Personal preferences from the repo owner for how the assistant should interact — tone, summary style, end-of-turn conventions, how results and decisions are surfaced, and the phrases the owner uses to trigger defined commands. These are **not** project conventions (those live in the [packs/](../packs/) corpus, each pack bundling its skills); they're how the owner likes to be worked with. Where a preference triggers a command whose mechanics are a project convention, the mechanics stay in their own doc and the entry here just owns the trigger phrase.

## Preferences

- **When there's nothing left to do, say so boldly at the end of the conversation.** Don't bury a "this is complete / I'm blocked / nothing further to do" behind hedging — make the terminal state unmistakable. **Set it apart visually so it catches the eye:** put the terminal state — or, when work remains, the list of open/outstanding items — in its own callout (a Markdown blockquote box, e.g. `> ✅ All done` or `> ⚠️ Still open: …`), not trailing prose the owner has to scan for. One or the other always gets the box: the done-signal when nothing remains, the open-items list when something does.
- **"LGTM"** is the owner's phrase for the merge-to-main command: it asks for the change in front of the owner to be merged into `main`. It's an owner-triggered command; the default mechanics live in the `merge-to-main` skill ([../packs/git-github/skills/merge-to-main/SKILL.md](../packs/git-github/skills/merge-to-main/SKILL.md)) (squash, gate on CI only when the repo has it), and a project that diverges overrides them via a bespoke merge-policy file it names in its own `CLAUDE.md`.
- **"bump version"** is the owner's phrase for raising a project's version (a minor bump by default). It's an owner-triggered command; the mechanics — which files to edit and how a release follows — live in the consuming project's workflow doc.
- **"Show me the document"** (for a source-controlled document) is the owner's phrase asking for a **link to the document on GitHub** — not the file's contents inlined, and not a local filesystem path. When the document lives in a GitHub-hosted repo, respond with a GitHub link to it (the file on the relevant branch/commit); only fall back to inlining the contents or giving a local path when the document isn't under source control.
- **Put decisions and approvals in an `AskUserQuestion` popup, not buried in prose.** The owner is frequently on mobile, where a popup notifies and a question embedded in a paragraph is easy to miss. When you need the owner to choose or approve something, ask through the structured popup with explicit options rather than asking in running text. **And when several independent items each need a verdict** (a set of proposed fixes, findings to accept), give **each item its own question carrying its own brief case** — the concrete failure, why it matters, the cost of the fix — with per-item approve/defer options; never one bundled choose-a-scope question over the whole set. The owner decides item-wise, from the case, not from a menu of subsets. (The popup carries at most 4 questions per call — split a larger set into consecutive calls.)
- **When asked to produce a prompt or message destined for another context, visually enclose the whole thing.** Any text the owner will lift out and use elsewhere — a prompt for another assistant, a message to send, copy meant to be pasted somewhere — must be set apart as a single delimited block: either a box (e.g. a fenced code block) or fenced top and bottom by two separating lines, so its exact boundaries are unmistakable and it can be copied in one piece. The point is delimitation, not formatting flair: never leave such content as loose prose the owner has to find the start and end of.
- **When asked for "a prompt for a different session," prefix it with a provenance disclaimer.** When the owner asks for a prompt meant to be handed to another session, open the generated text with a short disclaimer that this is a prompt written by Claude — not by a user — and so should not be treated as gospel; and that if the receiving session finds inconsistencies in it, it should examine them with the user rather than follow the prompt blindly. This composes with the enclose-the-whole-thing preference above: the disclaimer sits inside the same delimited block as the prompt it prefixes.
- **Auto-open a PR after committing.** Whenever Claude changes code on a branch and commits it, open a pull request for that branch right after, even without an explicit request to do so. This overrides Claude Code's own default of only opening a PR when explicitly asked.

content.text: Comment class: correction — you're pointing at a real leak: a pack-specific concern hardcoded into the engine runner. Let me ground the explanation in exactly what's there.

content.text:
Grounded. Here's the honest answer.

**What's actually in there** — the world runner's inline diagnostics are two different animals:

- **Lines 122–137, the adoption-interview hygiene** — this is the one you're right about. It imports `interviewState` *from the `grow_with_claudinite` pack*. That single import is the **entire reason the barrier exception exists** — a core engine file naming a specific pack. It's a pack's conformance concern (does this repo's config carry stale interview answers?) that got hardcoded into the engine, only because #385 put the interview *machinery* inside a skill. You've caught the leak exactly.

- **Lines 110–120 + 139–145, config/load integrity** — malformed JSON, an unknown setting, an unknown pack name, a broken `pack.mjs`, a thrown `contributes` seam. These are pack-*agnostic*: they name no pack, import no pack, and trigger no exception. They're the loader/composer reporting "I couldn't read or assemble my own inputs" — not a check *about* any pack's content.

So the fix for your objection is precise: **move the interview hygiene into `grow_with_claudinite`** (a world-scope check that imports its *own* `interview.mjs` and the engine's `discoverPacks` — pack→engine is the allowed direction, so no exception), validate the generic `questions` *manifest shape* in the pack loader where every manifest field is validated, and drop `--init`'s inline interview print (the adopt skill already drives the interview). Then the engine imports **no pack**, the barrier exception is **deleted**, and the sweep is what you described: run every active pack's world rules.

That leaves one genuine question — the pack-agnostic config/load integrity (the second bullet). Two decisions:

The generic `questions` manifest-shape validation moves into the pack loader. --init's inline interview print is dropped (the adopt skill drives it). Engine imports no pack; barrier exception deleted; the check runs only when that pack is active.

I have the full picture. This is a multi-file refactor; let me lay out the concrete plan and execute it methodically, testing at the end.

**Plan:** (1) move the generic `questions`-shape validator into the pack loader so a malformed `questions` surfaces as an ordinary `packErrors` load fault (engine-level, pack-agnostic); (2) the interview *hygiene* (stale answers) becomes a `grow_with_claudinite` skill-owned world check reading `ctx.packs`; (3) `check_the_world` imports no pack, `--init` drops its interview print; (4) delete the barrier exception.

gitDiff.patch:
@@ -0,0 +1,37 @@
+import { finding } from '../../../../engine/checks/helpers/findings.mjs';
+import { interviewState } from './interview.mjs';
+
+// Adoption-interview hygiene, owned by the pack that owns the interview. A STORED
+// answer whose question the pack no longer declares (renamed or removed upstream)
+// is a stale intent that silently stops matching — advisory, never run-failing,
+// so a canon-side question rename can't fail the fleet's CI overnight. This is a
+// grow_with_claudinite concern, not the engine's: it rides this pack's activation
+// like any skill-owned check, so a repo without the lifecycle pack never runs it
+// (and the engine imports no pack to do so).
+//
+// PENDING questions are deliberately NOT a finding — they surface only as the
+// mild SessionStart note (interview.mjs `check`), so an unattended run is never
+// blocked on a question nobody is present to answer. A MALFORMED `questions`
+// declaration is a manifest load fault the pack registry reports, not here.
+//
+// Reads ctx.packs (the discovered pack objects the runner attaches) so the check
+// runs synchronously without re-discovering packs.
+const rule = {
+  id: 'interview-answer-stale',
+  severity: 'advisory',
+  description: 'A stored adoption-interview answer names a question its pack still declares',
+  doc: 'packs/README.md',
+  why: 'a stale answer silently stops matching its question, so the stored intent goes unread and the interview re-asks',
+
+  run(ctx) {
+    if (!ctx.packs) return [];
+    const { stale } = interviewState(ctx.packs, ctx.config);
+    return stale.map(({ packId, answerId }) => finding(rule, {
+      file: '.claudinite-checks.json',
+      what: `the "${packId}" pack entry stores an answer for "${answerId}", a question the pack no longer declares`,
+      fix: 'remove the stale answer, or re-key it to the renamed question id',
+    }));
+  },
+};
+
+export default [rule];

-    what: `the "${s.packId}" pack entry stores an answer for "${s.answerId}", a question the pack no longer declares`,
-    why: 'a stale answer silently stops matching its question, so the stored intent goes unread and the interview re-asks',
-    fix: 'remove the stale answer, or re-key it to the renamed question id',
+// (Adoption-interview hygiene is a grow_with_claudinite skill-owned check, run
+// below with the other active-pack rules; a malformed `questions` field arrives
+// as a load fault in packErrors above. Neither needs a pack import here.)
// The world rules: everything not scoped to the work. A broken contributedRules
// seam is a config-level fault surfaced here (the world runner owns diagnostics).

The barriers exception for check_the_world.mjs is deleted — the runner names
no pack, and the barrier check actively enforces that (a pack mention, even
in a comment, is flagged BLOCKING). SessionStart's interview-check step is

input.body:
Closes #392.

## What

Reworks Claudinite's conformance checks into **two fully independent scope runners** that fire at **different times**, gives the check-the-work rules a fluent context, and strips all pack-specific machinery out of the engine runners.

### Fluent `work` context
- `engine/checks/helpers/work.mjs` — a null-safe fluent view over `ctx`. A rule declares `scope: 'work'` and its signature becomes `run(work)`; `runRule(rule, ctx)` is the single dispatch seam. API: `work.conversation().ownerTurns().last()` (null-object turn — no guard ladders), `work.jsonPair(file)`, `work.deadLinks()`, `work.danglingReferences(tolerated)`, `work.addedLines()`, `work.branchCommits()`, `work.introducedMerges()`, `work.filesContaining()`, plus delegated raw surface. Full `work`/`lastOwnerTurn`/`lastFeatureTurn` naming, no abbreviations.
- `ctx.read` / `ctx.readBase` cached per sweep.
- Eight work rules converted to `scope: 'work'` / `run(work)`.

### Two independent runners, no shared pipeline
`check_the_world.mjs` and `check_the_work.mjs` are fully independent — **neither imports the other, and neither names any pack.** They share only two scope-blind mechanism helpers:
- `run-active-pack-rules.mjs` — run the active packs' rules a predicate admits (also attaches the discovered packs to `ctx.packs` for rules that reason about pack metadata).
- `report-findings.mjs` — apply config, sort, print, return the blocking count.

### The engine names no pack
The world runner used to import the adoption-interview machinery from a specific pack — the sole reason for a `barriers` core/content exception. That's gone:
- The generic `questions` **manifest-shape validator** moved into the **pack loader**, so a malformed `questions` field is an ordinary load fault in `packErrors`, reported pack-agnostically.
- The interview **hygiene** (a stored answer whose question its pack no longer declares) is now a **skill-owned world check** in the lifecycle pack's `adopt-claudinite` skill, reading `ctx.packs`. It rides that pack's activation like any skill check — a repo without the lifecycle pack never runs it.
- `--init` no longer prints pending questions (the adopt skill + the SessionStart interview-check step drive the interview).
- The `barriers` exception for `check_the_world.mjs` is **deleted** — and the barrier check actively enforces the boundary (a pack mention, even in a comment, is flagged BLOCKING; verified by mutation).

The world runner's only non-rule work now is **pack-agnostic config/load integrity** (malformed JSON, unknown setting/pack, broken `pack.mjs`, thrown `contributes` seam) — the loader reporting it couldn't assemble its inputs, naming no pack.

### Different timings
- **Work → the Stop hook.** `stop-command.mjs` runs `check_the_work.mjs` only — the per-turn feedback loop, with the transcript.
- **World → the project's test/CI flow**, as a standalone `node …/check_the_world.mjs` step (not a language-specific test file — it's the engine's always-vendored Node CLI). Bootstrap wires it in, adding a minimal flow where the repo has none. **CI runs the world sweep only.**
- This **supersedes the original #385 decision** (Stop as the whole enforcement surface, no consumer CI). DESIGN.md, `engine/checks/README.md`, `bootstrap.md`, the `adopt-claudinite` skill, and the top-level README are updated; canon `ci.yml` models the pattern.

## Verification
- Full suite green throughout: **491 tests** (added `engine-tests/hooks/stop-command.test.mjs`).
- See-it-fail by mutation at each step: `Turns.last`, `danglingReferences`, `jsonPair`, the `runRule` scope dispatch, the Stop-hook work-only behavior, the barrier guard on the work runner's independence, the interview-hygiene skill check running via the runner, and the loader's malformed-`questions` validation each made a check bite; every mutation restored byte-identical.
- End-to-end: on a fixture carrying both a world (`warning-suppression`) and a work (`reference-integrity`) violation — `check_the_world` reports only the world one, `check_the_work` only the work ones, and the Stop hook surfaces only work. `--list`/`--init` intact. Both sweeps run clean on this repo (0 blocking); the world runner names no pack.

Also removed three broken `.claude/skills/` symlinks (pre-#385 layout leftovers).

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)

https://claude.ai/code/session_015EGXi3y8hZNfS7A9csREv1

content.text: {"id":"4107354760","url":"https://github.com/missingbulb/Claudinite/pull/393"}

Comment class: correction — you caught a pack-specific mechanism hardcoded into the engine runner.

You were right. `check_the_world` was importing the adoption-interview machinery *from a specific pack* to emit its diagnostics — and that one import was the entire reason for the barrier exception you kept seeing. A pack's concern living in the engine, exactly as you said it shouldn't.

Now the world runner does what you described — run every active pack's world checks — and **names no pack at all**:

- **Malformed `questions`** → moved to the pack loader, so it's an ordinary load fault (`packErrors`), reported pack-agnostically like a broken `pack.mjs`. This is the "config/load integrity" you said stays engine-level.
- **Interview hygiene** (stale answers) → now a **skill-owned world check** in the lifecycle pack's `adopt-claudinite` skill, reading the packs the runner attaches to `ctx.packs`. It runs only when that pack is active — a repo without it never runs the check, and the engine imports nothing.
- **`--init`** stopped printing pending questions; the adopt skill and the SessionStart step already drive the interview.
- **The barrier exception is deleted** — and the barrier check now actively enforces it: a pack mention in `check_the_world`, *even in a comment*, is flagged BLOCKING (it caught my own explanatory comments; I reworded them).

The only non-rule thing the world runner still does is pack-agnostic config/load integrity (malformed JSON, unknown pack, broken `pack.mjs`) — the loader reporting it couldn't assemble its inputs, naming no pack. Per your earlier call, that stays engine-level.

491 green; both sweeps clean; world runner references zero packs. All four new behaviors see-it-fail verified. Pushed to [PR #393](https://github.com/missingbulb/Claudinite/pull/393).

> ✅ All done — the interview leak is gone, `check_the_world` names no pack, the barrier exception is deleted (and barrier-enforced), and the engine just runs the packs' world checks. Say "LGTM" to merge, or point me at the next thing.

# Owner interaction preferences

Personal preferences from the repo owner for how the assistant should interact — tone, summary style, end-of-turn conventions, how results and decisions are surfaced, and the phrases the owner uses to trigger defined commands. These are **not** project conventions (those live in the [packs/](../packs/) corpus, each pack bundling its skills); they're how the owner likes to be worked with. Where a preference triggers a command whose mechanics are a project convention, the mechanics stay in their own doc and the entry here just owns the trigger phrase.

## Preferences

- **When there's nothing left to do, say so boldly at the end of the conversation.** Don't bury a "this is complete / I'm blocked / nothing further to do" behind hedging — make the terminal state unmistakable. **Set it apart visually so it catches the eye:** put the terminal state — or, when work remains, the list of open/outstanding items — in its own callout (a Markdown blockquote box, e.g. `> ✅ All done` or `> ⚠️ Still open: …`), not trailing prose the owner has to scan for. One or the other always gets the box: the done-signal when nothing remains, the open-items list when something does.
- **"LGTM"** is the owner's phrase for the merge-to-main command: it asks for the change in front of the owner to be merged into `main`. It's an owner-triggered command; the default mechanics live in the `merge-to-main` skill ([../packs/git-github/skills/merge-to-main/SKILL.md](../packs/git-github/skills/merge-to-main/SKILL.md)) (squash, gate on CI only when the repo has it), and a project that diverges overrides them via a bespoke merge-policy file it names in its own `CLAUDE.md`.
- **"bump version"** is the owner's phrase for raising a project's version (a minor bump by default). It's an owner-triggered command; the mechanics — which files to edit and how a release follows — live in the consuming project's workflow doc.
- **"Show me the document"** (for a source-controlled document) is the owner's phrase asking for a **link to the document on GitHub** — not the file's contents inlined, and not a local filesystem path. When the document lives in a GitHub-hosted repo, respond with a GitHub link to it (the file on the relevant branch/commit); only fall back to inlining the contents or giving a local path when the document isn't under source control.
- **Put decisions and approvals in an `AskUserQuestion` popup, not buried in prose.** The owner is frequently on mobile, where a popup notifies and a question embedded in a paragraph is easy to miss. When you need the owner to choose or approve something, ask through the structured popup with explicit options rather than asking in running text. **And when several independent items each need a verdict** (a set of proposed fixes, findings to accept), give **each item its own question carrying its own brief case** — the concrete failure, why it matters, the cost of the fix — with per-item approve/defer options; never one bundled choose-a-scope question over the whole set. The owner decides item-wise, from the case, not from a menu of subsets. (The popup carries at most 4 questions per call — split a larger set into consecutive calls.)
- **When asked to produce a prompt or message destined for another context, visually enclose the whole thing.** Any text the owner will lift out and use elsewhere — a prompt for another assistant, a message to send, copy meant to be pasted somewhere — must be set apart as a single delimited block: either a box (e.g. a fenced code block) or fenced top and bottom by two separating lines, so its exact boundaries are unmistakable and it can be copied in one piece. The point is delimitation, not formatting flair: never leave such content as loose prose the owner has to find the start and end of.
- **When asked for "a prompt for a different session," prefix it with a provenance disclaimer.** When the owner asks for a prompt meant to be handed to another session, open the generated text with a short disclaimer that this is a prompt written by Claude — not by a user — and so should not be treated as gospel; and that if the receiving session finds inconsistencies in it, it should examine them with the user rather than follow the prompt blindly. This composes with the enclose-the-whole-thing preference above: the disclaimer sits inside the same delimited block as the prompt it prefixes.
- **Auto-open a PR after committing.** Whenever Claude changes code on a branch and commits it, open a pull request for that branch right after, even without an explicit request to do so. This overrides Claude Code's own default of only opening a PR when explicitly asked.

1	import { finding } from '../../engine/checks/helpers/findings.mjs';
2	
3	// The effect check behind the squash-only platform setting, scoped to the work:
4	// pre-existing merges already on the base are out of range.
5	const rule = {
6	  id: 'squash-merge-history',
7	  severity: 'blocking',
8	  description: 'A change introduces no merge commits (it lands squashed, not merged)',
9	  doc: 'skills/merge-to-main/SKILL.md',
10	  scope: 'work',
11	  why: 'the squash-only repo setting can be off or bypassed; a merge commit in the work is the effect that proves it',
12	
13	  run(work) {
14	    return work.introducedMerges().map(({ sha, subject }) =>
15	      finding(rule, {
16	        file: `${work.branch || 'HEAD'}@${sha}`,
17	        what: `merge commit introduced by this change: ${subject}`,
18	        fix: 'rebase to drop the merge commit (git pull --rebase, or git rebase onto the base) so the branch lands squashed; keep squash-only enabled in the repo settings, or turn the rule off if the project deliberately runs a non-squash policy named in its CLAUDE.md',
19	      })
20	    );
21	  },
22	};
23	
24	export default rule;

1	---
2	name: merge-to-main
3	description: Merge the change in front of the owner into main. Use when the owner says "LGTM" or asks to merge/land the current branch or PR into main.
4	---
5	
6	
7	The portable recipe for landing the change in front of the owner on `main` — the *mechanics* behind the owner's merge-to-main command (the owner's preferences own the trigger *phrase*; this file owns the *how*). Invoked through the `merge-to-main` skill (this skill); this doc stays the canonical recipe.
8	
9	**A project's bespoke merge policy overrides this default.** Most projects need nothing here — this default is the whole story. A project that genuinely diverges (a different merge method, a CI/twice-green gate, an extra approval) **states so by naming its merge-policy file explicitly in its own `CLAUDE.md`**. When that declaration is present, read that file first and let it override the divergent points below; when it's absent, follow this default as-is. Don't go hunting for a policy file that the project's `CLAUDE.md` doesn't name.
10	
11	## Recipe (~4 calls)
12	
13	1. Load both GitHub tools in **one** `ToolSearch`: `create_pull_request` + `merge_pull_request`.
14	2. No PR open for the branch yet? `create_pull_request` (base `main`); end the body with `Closes #<issue>`, and take the PR number from the returned URL.
15	3. Gate on CI **only if the repo has it** — a workflow that actually runs on PRs/pushes. `workflow_call`-only reusable workflows (this repo hosts some) are *not* CI: they never run here, so a non-empty `.github/workflows/` alone proves nothing; check the PR for check runs instead. No CI → no gate; don't wait for checks that will never come.
16	4. `merge_pull_request`, `merge_method: squash`, title `<subject> (#<pr>)`. Merge directly — don't pre-read status; the call fails loudly if it isn't mergeable.
17	5. Sync local `main`: `git checkout main && git pull origin main`.
18	
19	The two divergent points — **`squash`** as the method and **CI gating** — are exactly what a bespoke policy file changes.
20	
21	## After the merge: capture the conversation (every session, every user)
22	
23	Once the merge has landed and local `main` is synced, run the growth pack's capture step:
24	`node .claudinite/shared/packs/grow_with_claudinite/capture-log.mjs --issue <n>` (from the canon repo itself: `node packs/grow_with_claudinite/capture-log.mjs --issue <n>`), `<n>` being the issue your `Closes #<issue>` named. Deterministic, seconds-long; it pushes the conversation to the orphan `conversation-logs` branch. It runs **here, in-session, because it needs the live transcript** — the lessons extraction then happens later in the fleet's `conversation-extract` run_daily task (central, MCP-native, with a rethink window), so there is nothing to schedule and no in-session lessons pass ([the pack README](../../../../packs/grow_with_claudinite/README.md) owns the standard). A later merge in the same session just runs capture again — it captures the delta. In the rare repo that removed the `grow_with_claudinite` pack from `.claudinite-checks.json`, skip this step.
25	
26	## Don't
27	
28	- **Don't** re-read the issue to confirm it closed — `Closes #<issue>` does that on merge; trust it.
29

file.content:
---
name: merge-to-main
description: Merge the change in front of the owner into main. Use when the owner says "LGTM" or asks to merge/land the current branch or PR into main.
---


The portable recipe for landing the change in front of the owner on `main` — the *mechanics* behind the owner's merge-to-main command (the owner's preferences own the trigger *phrase*; this file owns the *how*). Invoked through the `merge-to-main` skill (this skill); this doc stays the canonical recipe.

**A project's bespoke merge policy overrides this default.** Most projects need nothing here — this default is the whole story. A project that genuinely diverges (a different merge method, a CI/twice-green gate, an extra approval) **states so by naming its merge-policy file explicitly in its own `CLAUDE.md`**. When that declaration is present, read that file first and let it override the divergent points below; when it's absent, follow this default as-is. Don't go hunting for a policy file that the project's `CLAUDE.md` doesn't name.

## Recipe (~4 calls)

1. Load both GitHub tools in **one** `ToolSearch`: `create_pull_request` + `merge_pull_request`.
2. No PR open for the branch yet? `create_pull_request` (base `main`); end the body with `Closes #<issue>`, and take the PR number from the returned URL.
3. Gate on CI **only if the repo has it** — a workflow that actually runs on PRs/pushes. `workflow_call`-only reusable workflows (this repo hosts some) are *not* CI: they never run here, so a non-empty `.github/workflows/` alone proves nothing; check the PR for check runs instead. No CI → no gate; don't wait for checks that will never come.
4. `merge_pull_request`, `merge_method: squash`, title `<subject> (#<pr>)`. Merge directly — don't pre-read status; the call fails loudly if it isn't mergeable.
5. Sync local `main`: `git checkout main && git pull origin main`.

The two divergent points — **`squash`** as the method and **CI gating** — are exactly what a bespoke policy file changes.

## After the merge: capture the conversation (every session, every user)

Once the merge has landed and local `main` is synced, run the growth pack's capture step:
`node .claudinite/shared/packs/grow_with_claudinite/capture-log.mjs --issue <n>` (from the canon repo itself: `node packs/grow_with_claudinite/capture-log.mjs --issue <n>`), `<n>` being the issue your `Closes #<issue>` named. Deterministic, seconds-long; it pushes the conversation to the orphan `conversation-logs` branch. It runs **here, in-session, because it needs the live transcript** — the lessons extraction then happens later in the fleet's `conversation-extract` run_daily task (central, MCP-native, with a rethink window), so there is nothing to schedule and no in-session lessons pass ([the pack README](../../../../packs/grow_with_claudinite/README.md) owns the standard). A later merge in the same session just runs capture again — it captures the delta. In the rare repo that removed the `grow_with_claudinite` pack from `.claudinite-checks.json`, skip this step.

## Don't

- **Don't** re-read the issue to confirm it closed — `Closes #<issue>` does that on merge; trust it.
